egypt.name: Egypt
egypt.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes: red (top), white (middle), black (bottom), with a golden eagle in the center
egypt.famousFor: The Great Pyramids, the Sphinx, and the Nile River

geography.longestRiver: The Nile — the longest river in the world at 4,130 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Catherine — 8,625 feet

famousFood: Koshari — a mix of rice, lentils, pasta, and spicy tomato sauce
Ful Medames — mashed fava beans, a breakfast staple for thousands of years
Basbousa — sweet semolina cake soaked in syrup
festivals: Sham el-Nessim — a spring festival celebrated for over 4,500 years, one of the oldest holidays in the world
famousPeople: Cleopatra — last pharaoh of ancient Egypt
Mohamed Salah — world-famous soccer star
Hatshepsut — one of the most powerful female pharaohs
inventions: Papyrus (early paper)
Toothpaste
The 365-day calendar
Black ink for writing
worldRecords: The Great Pyramid was the tallest structure on Earth for 3,800 years
The Nile is the longest river in the world
weirdLaws: In ancient Egypt, killing a cat — even accidentally — could be punished by death
uniqueAnimals: Egyptian Vulture
Fennec Fox — the world's smallest fox with huge ears
sportsAchievements: Egypt has won the Africa Cup of Nations 7 times — more than any other country
Mohamed Salah won the Premier League Golden Boot
history: Ancient Egypt lasted over 3,000 years — longer than any other civilization!
The Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest building in the world for 3,800 years.
Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the building of the pyramids.
Ancient Egyptians invented paper-like sheets called papyrus from river reeds.
trivia: Ancient Egyptians loved cats so much that hurting one was a serious crime.
They invented toothpaste — made from crushed eggshells and ox hooves!
Both men and women in ancient Egypt wore makeup, especially around their eyes.

greece.name: Greece
greece.flagDescription: Nine horizontal stripes alternating blue and white, with a white cross on a blue square in the upper left corner
greece.famousFor: The birthplace of democracy, the Olympic Games, and Greek mythology
landmarks: Mount Olympus — home of the Greek gods

geography.longestRiver: Aliakmonas River — 185 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Olympus — 9,573 feet (where the ancient Greeks believed the gods lived)

famousFood: Gyros — meat wrapped in warm pita bread
Moussaka — baked layers of eggplant, meat, and creamy sauce
Baklava — sweet pastry with nuts and honey
festivals: Ancient Olympic Games — the original Olympics started here in 776 BC
famousPeople: Socrates — one of the founders of Western philosophy
Alexander the Great — conquered most of the known world by age 30
Archimedes — legendary mathematician and inventor
inventions: Democracy
The Olympic Games
Western philosophy
The Hippocratic Oath (medicine)
Theater and drama
worldRecords: Greece has more archaeological museums than any other country in the world
Athens is one of the oldest cities in the world — over 3,400 years old
weirdLaws: In ancient Sparta, it was illegal for men to live a luxury lifestyle — even their soup was intentionally bad!
uniqueAnimals: Mediterranean Monk Seal — one of the rarest seals in the world
Kri-Kri — a wild goat found only in Crete
sportsAchievements: Greece won the 2004 European soccer championship as a massive underdog — one of the biggest upsets in sports history
history: Ancient Greece invented democracy — the idea that people get to vote on their leaders.
The first Olympic Games were held in 776 BC in Olympia, Greece.
Alexander the Great conquered most of the known world by age 30.
Ancient Greeks built the Parthenon temple, which still stands in Athens today.
trivia: Greece has more than 6,000 islands, but people only live on about 227 of them.
The ancient Greeks thought beans held the souls of dead people!
The word 'dinosaur' comes from Greek and means 'terrible lizard.'

china.name: China
china.flagDescription: Red background with five yellow stars in the upper left — one large star and four smaller ones
china.famousFor: The Great Wall, ancient inventions, and being one of the oldest civilizations

geography.longestRiver: Yangtze River — 3,917 miles, the longest river in Asia and third longest in the world
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Everest — 29,032 feet (shared with Nepal), the tallest mountain on Earth

famousFood: Peking Duck — crispy roasted duck, a Beijing specialty for centuries
Dim Sum — small dishes of dumplings, buns, and rolls
Kung Pao Chicken — spicy stir-fried chicken with peanuts
festivals: Chinese New Year — the biggest celebration in China, lasting 15 days with fireworks, dragon dances, and red envelopes
famousPeople: Confucius — philosopher whose ideas shaped China for 2,500 years
Zheng He — explorer who sailed massive fleets across Asia and Africa
Yao Ming — NBA basketball star
inventions: Paper
Printing
Gunpowder
The Compass
Silk
Fireworks
worldRecords: The Great Wall is the longest structure ever built — over 13,000 miles
China has the world's largest population
weirdLaws: In ancient China, only the emperor was allowed to wear yellow — anyone else could be punished!
uniqueAnimals: Giant Panda — found only in China, they eat bamboo for up to 14 hours a day
Chinese Giant Salamander — the world's largest amphibian, up to 6 feet long
Golden Snub-Nosed Monkey
sportsAchievements: China has dominated Olympic table tennis, winning 32 gold medals
China hosted the 2008 Summer Olympics in Beijing
history: The Great Wall of China is over 13,000 miles long — that's halfway around the Earth!
China invented paper, printing, gunpowder, and the compass.
The Terracotta Army has over 8,000 life-sized clay soldiers buried to guard an emperor.
Chinese civilization is over 5,000 years old, one of the oldest in the world.
trivia: Fortune cookies were actually invented in California, not China!
China uses 45 billion chopsticks every year.
The Chinese New Year celebration lasts 15 days.

india.name: India
india.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes: saffron (top), white (middle), green (bottom), with a blue wheel (Ashoka Chakra) in the center
india.famousFor: The Taj Mahal, spices, Bollywood, and ancient mathematics
landmarks: Varanasi — one of the oldest cities in the world

geography.longestRiver: Ganges River — 1,569 miles, considered sacred by Hindus
geography.tallestPeak: Kangchenjunga — 28,169 feet, the third tallest mountain in the world

famousFood: Biryani — fragrant rice dish with spices and meat
Butter Chicken — creamy tomato curry
Samosas — crispy fried pastries filled with spiced potatoes
festivals: Diwali — the Festival of Lights, celebrated by millions with lamps, fireworks, and sweets
Holi — the Festival of Colors, where people throw colored powder at each other
famousPeople: Mahatma Gandhi — led India's independence through nonviolent protest
Aryabhata — ancient mathematician who helped develop the concept of zero
Sachin Tendulkar — one of the greatest cricket players of all time
inventions: The number zero
Chess
Buttons
Shampoo (from the Hindi word 'champo')
The decimal system
worldRecords: India has the world's largest movie industry (Bollywood — 1,500+ films per year)
India has the world's largest school with 55,000 students
weirdLaws: In some parts of India, cows have their own ID cards and passports!
uniqueAnimals: Bengal Tiger
Indian Elephant
Indian Rhinoceros
Asiatic Lion — found only in the Gir Forest of India
sportsAchievements: India has won the Cricket World Cup twice (1983 and 2011)
India invented the sport of polo
history: The Indus Valley Civilization (in modern India/Pakistan) is one of the world's oldest, dating back 5,000 years.
India invented the number zero — without it, modern math and computers wouldn't exist!
The Taj Mahal took 22 years and 20,000 workers to build.
India was the world's largest producer of spices for thousands of years, attracting explorers from everywhere.
trivia: India has the largest movie industry in the world — Bollywood makes over 1,500 films a year!
Chess was invented in India around 1,500 years ago.
India is home to the world's largest school, with over 55,000 students.

japan.name: Japan
japan.flagDescription: White background with a large red circle in the center, representing the sun
japan.famousFor: Samurai warriors, anime, technology, and cherry blossoms
landmarks: Fushimi Inari Shrine with 10,000 red gates

geography.tallestPeak: Mount Fuji — 12,388 feet, an iconic volcanic peak

famousFood: Sushi — vinegared rice with raw fish
Ramen — noodle soup with rich broth
Tempura — lightly battered and fried seafood and vegetables
festivals: Cherry Blossom Festival (Hanami) — millions gather under blooming cherry trees each spring
famousPeople: Hokusai — artist who painted 'The Great Wave,' one of the most famous artworks ever
Akira Kurosawa — legendary film director
Shohei Ohtani — MLB superstar who pitches AND hits
inventions: Bullet Train (Shinkansen)
Nintendo and PlayStation
Instant Ramen
Emojis
Blu-ray discs
worldRecords: Tokyo is the most populated city in the world (37 million people)

weirdLaws: In Japan, it's considered rude to blow your nose in public — sniffing is preferred!
uniqueAnimals: Japanese Macaque (Snow Monkey) — they bathe in hot springs in winter!
Tanuki (Raccoon Dog)
Giant Japanese Spider Crab — the largest crab in the world
sportsAchievements: Japan hosted the Olympics three times (1964, 2020 Tokyo, and 1972 Winter)
Japan's baseball league is one of the best outside the US
history: Samurai warriors followed a strict code of honor called Bushido for over 700 years.
Japan was completely isolated from the rest of the world for over 200 years (1603–1868).
Tokyo was originally a small fishing village called Edo before becoming the capital.
Japan rebuilt itself into a technology powerhouse after World War II.
trivia: Japan has more than 5.5 million vending machines — one for every 23 people!
There's an island in Japan called Ōkunoshima that's full of hundreds of wild rabbits.
In Japan, there are more pets than children.

usa.name: United States

geography.tallestPeak: Denali (Mount McKinley) in Alaska — 20,310 feet, tallest peak in North America

festivals: Fourth of July — Independence Day with fireworks, parades, and cookouts
Thanksgiving — a holiday centered on gratitude and a big feast
famousPeople: Abraham Lincoln — president who ended slavery
Neil Armstrong — first person to walk on the Moon
Martin Luther King Jr. — civil rights leader
Albert Einstein — developed his famous theories while living in the US
inventions: The airplane (Wright Brothers)
The internet
The light bulb (Thomas Edison)
The telephone (Alexander Graham Bell)
GPS
worldRecords: The US has won more Olympic medals than any country in history (2,600+)

uniqueAnimals: Bald Eagle
American Bison — once nearly extinct, now making a comeback

sportsAchievements: The US has won the most Olympic gold medals in history

history: The Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776, making the USA one of the first modern democracies.

The Statue of Liberty was a gift from France in 1886.
The USA started as just 13 colonies and grew to 50 states.
trivia: Alaska is the largest US state — it's bigger than Texas, California, and Montana combined!
The US has no official language at the federal level.
There are more public libraries in the US than McDonald's restaurants.

Neymar — soccer superstar

Toucan — with its giant colorful beak

trivia: Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup 5 times — more than any other country!
The Amazon River has river dolphins that are pink!

famousPeople: Napoleon Bonaparte — conquered much of Europe
Marie Curie — discovered radioactivity, first woman to win a Nobel Prize
Claude Monet — founded the Impressionist art movement
inventions: The hot air balloon
Braille (reading system for blind people)
Pasteurization (making milk safe to drink)
The metric system
worldRecords: France is the most visited country in the world (∼90 million tourists per year)

weirdLaws: French law says you cannot name a pig 'Napoleon'

uniqueAnimals: Camargue Horse — beautiful white wild horses in southern France

history: The French Revolution in 1789 overthrew the king and changed how the world thinks about freedom.

Napoleon Bonaparte conquered much of Europe before being defeated at Waterloo.
France gave the Statue of Liberty to the United States as a gift.
trivia: France is the most visited country in the world — about 90 million tourists per year!
The Louvre Museum in Paris has over 380,000 artworks, including the Mona Lisa.
French law says you cannot name a pig 'Napoleon.'

mexico.name: Mexico

geography.tallestPeak: Pico de Orizaba — 18,491 feet, the highest volcano in North America

famousFood: Tacos — soft tortillas with meat, salsa, and toppings
Mole — a rich sauce made with chocolate and chili peppers
Tamales — corn dough filled with meat, wrapped in corn husks
festivals: Day of the Dead (Día de los Muertos) — a beautiful celebration to remember loved ones with colorful altars, marigolds, and sugar skulls

Guillermo del Toro — Oscar-winning film director

Chocolate (originally an Aztec drink)

Mexico introduced chocolate, tomatoes, and chili peppers to the rest of the world.

The Day of the Dead is a celebration to remember loved ones who have passed away.

australia.name: Australia

australia.famousFor: Unique wildlife, the Great Barrier Reef, Aboriginal culture, and the Outback

geography.tallestPeak: Mount Kosciuszko — 7,310 feet (Australia is the flattest continent)

famousFood: Vegemite — a dark, salty spread that Australians love on toast
Meat Pies — a classic Aussie snack
Lamingtons — sponge cake coated in chocolate and coconut
festivals: Australia Day (January 26) — national day with barbecues, fireworks, and beach events
famousPeople: Steve Irwin — 'The Crocodile Hunter,' famous wildlife expert
Hugh Jackman — actor known as Wolverine
Cathy Freeman — Olympic gold medalist sprinter and Indigenous Australian icon
inventions: Wi-Fi
The black box flight recorder
Google Maps (co-founded by Australians)
Plastic banknotes (money)
worldRecords: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth — visible from space

weirdLaws: It is illegal to walk on the right-hand side of the sidewalk in some Australian states

uniqueAnimals: Kangaroo
Koala

history: Aboriginal Australians have the oldest continuous culture on Earth — over 65,000 years!

Australia was originally used as a British penal colony — a place to send prisoners.
The Sydney Opera House took 16 years to build and opened in 1973.
trivia: Australia has more kangaroos than people — about 50 million kangaroos!

uk.name: United Kingdom
uk.flagDescription: A blue background with the red and white crosses of St. George, St. Andrew, and St. Patrick layered on top of each other, known as the Union Jack.
uk.famousFor: The birthplace of the English language, Shakespeare, and the world's oldest parliamentary democracy.
landmarks: Big Ben and the Houses of Parliament

geography.longestRiver: River Severn — 220 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Ben Nevis — 4,413 feet

famousFood: Fish and Chips — battered fried fish with thick-cut fries
Full English Breakfast — eggs, bacon, sausages, beans, and toast

famousPeople: William Shakespeare — greatest playwright in the English language

Telephone

In World War II, Britain stood alone against Nazi Germany during the Battle of Britain in 1940.
trivia: The Queen owned all unmarked swans in England and Wales — a tradition dating back to the 12th century.

germany.name: Germany
germany.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes of black on top, red in the middle, and gold on the bottom.
germany.famousFor: Engineering excellence, cars like BMW and Mercedes, and being the birthplace of many great scientists and musicians.

geography.tallestPeak: Zugspitze — 9,718 feet

famousFood: Bratwurst — grilled sausage served with mustard and bread

Black Forest Cake — chocolate cake with cherries and whipped cream
festivals: Oktoberfest — the world's largest folk festival held every year in Munich, attracting over 6 million visitors
famousPeople: Albert Einstein — developed the theory of relativity and changed our understanding of the universe

Automobile (Karl Benz)
Aspirin

European Bison — the largest land animal in Europe, reintroduced to German nature reserves
sportsAchievements: Germany has won the FIFA World Cup 4 times (1954, 1974, 1990, 2014)
Dirk Nowitzki became one of the greatest international NBA players of all time

Germany was at the center of both World War I and World War II in the 20th century.
The Holy Roman Empire, centered in Germany, lasted almost 1,000 years (800–1806 AD).
Germany reunified in 1990 after being split into two countries for over 40 years.

The Christmas tree tradition started in Germany.

italy.name: Italy
italy.flagDescription: Three vertical stripes of green on the left, white in the middle, and red on the right.

geography.longestRiver: Po River — 405 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mont Blanc (Monte Bianco) — 15,774 feet (shared with France)

Gelato — Italian-style ice cream that's smoother and denser than regular ice cream
festivals: Carnival of Venice — a world-famous festival where people wear elaborate masks and costumes

Lollipop (modern wrapped version)

russia.name: Russia

geography.tallestPeak: Mount Elbrus — 18,510 feet (highest peak in Europe)

famousFood: Borscht — a deep-red beet soup often served with sour cream
Pelmeni — small dumplings stuffed with meat, similar to ravioli
Blini — thin Russian pancakes served with jam, sour cream, or caviar
festivals: Maslenitsa — a week-long festival before Lent celebrating the end of winter with pancakes and bonfires
famousPeople: Yuri Gagarin — the first human to travel into outer space in 1961
Leo Tolstoy — author of War and Peace, one of the greatest novels ever written
Pyotr Tchaikovsky — composed The Nutcracker, Swan Lake, and other famous ballets
inventions: Space Satellite (Sputnik)
Helicopter (Igor Sikorsky)
Periodic Table of Elements (Dmitri Mendeleev)
worldRecords: Russia is the largest country in the world — 6.6 million square miles, nearly twice the size of Canada
Lake Baikal in Siberia is the deepest lake in the world at 5,387 feet deep
weirdLaws: In Russia, it is illegal to drive a dirty car in some cities — you can get fined
uniqueAnimals: Siberian Tiger — the largest cat in the world, found in Russia's far east
Baikal Seal (Nerpa) — the only freshwater seal in the world, living in Lake Baikal
Saiga Antelope — an odd-looking antelope with a droopy nose found in Russian steppes
sportsAchievements: Soviet and Russian athletes have won over 1,200 Olympic medals across all games
Russia dominated Olympic gymnastics and figure skating for decades
history: The Russian Empire was once one of the largest in history, stretching from Europe to the Pacific Ocean.
The Russian Revolution of 1917 overthrew the Tsar and created the Soviet Union.
During World War II, the Soviet Union played a critical role in defeating Nazi Germany.
The Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, and Russia became an independent nation again.
trivia: Russia has 11 time zones — when it's morning in one part, it's already nighttime in another.
The Moscow Metro is famous for its beautiful stations, decorated like palaces with chandeliers and murals.
Russia sent the first animal into orbit — a dog named Laika in 1957.

canada.name: Canada
canada.flagDescription: A red maple leaf in the center on a white background, with red bars on the left and right sides.
canada.famousFor: Stunning natural landscapes, maple syrup, and being one of the friendliest countries in the world.

geography.longestRiver: Mackenzie River — 2,635 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Logan — 19,551 feet

famousFood: Poutine — french fries topped with cheese curds and hot gravy
Maple Syrup — Canada produces about 71% of the world's maple syrup
Butter Tarts — a sweet pastry filled with butter, sugar, and eggs
festivals: Calgary Stampede — one of the world's largest rodeos and western festivals
famousPeople: Wayne Gretzky — considered the greatest ice hockey player of all time
Terry Fox — ran across Canada on one leg to raise money for cancer research
James Naismith — a Canadian who invented basketball
inventions: Basketball
Insulin (life-saving diabetes treatment)
Peanut Butter
worldRecords: Canada has the longest coastline of any country — over 151,000 miles
Canada has more lakes than all other countries combined
weirdLaws: In parts of Canada, it was once illegal to pay for something with too many coins (the Currency Act limits coin payments)
uniqueAnimals: Moose — the largest member of the deer family, standing up to 7 feet tall
Polar Bear — Canada is home to about two-thirds of the world's polar bears
Narwhal — the unicorn of the sea, found in Canada's Arctic waters
sportsAchievements: Canada has won the most Olympic gold medals in ice hockey
The Toronto Raptors won the NBA Championship in 2019
history: Indigenous peoples lived in Canada for over 15,000 years before Europeans arrived.
Both France and England colonized parts of Canada, which is why the country has two official languages.
Canada became an independent country (Dominion) on July 1, 1867 — celebrated as Canada Day.
During the Underground Railroad, Canada was a safe destination for enslaved people escaping from the United States.
trivia: Canada is the second-largest country in the world by area, but has fewer people than the state of California.
The Royal Canadian Mounted Police (Mounties) are famous for their red uniforms and wide-brimmed hats.
Alert, Nunavut is the northernmost permanent settlement in the world — only 508 miles from the North Pole.

argentina.name: Argentina
argentina.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes — light blue on top, white in the middle with a golden sun (Sol de Mayo), and light blue on the bottom.
argentina.nationalSport: Pato (a game played on horseback, like a mix of polo and basketball)
argentina.famousFor: Tango dancing, amazing steak, football legends, and the stunning Patagonia wilderness.
landmarks: Casa Rosada (the Pink House) in Buenos Aires

geography.longestRiver: Paraná River — 3,032 miles (total length)
geography.tallestPeak: Aconcagua — 22,837 feet (highest peak in the Americas)

famousFood: Asado — a traditional Argentine barbecue with beef cooked slowly over an open flame
Empanadas — pastry pockets stuffed with meat, cheese, or vegetables
Dulce de Leche — a caramel-like spread made from sweetened milk, used on everything
festivals: Tango Festival in Buenos Aires — the world's biggest tango event with dancers from all over the globe
famousPeople: Lionel Messi — widely considered the greatest football (soccer) player of all time
Diego Maradona — legendary footballer famous for the 'Hand of God' goal
Eva Perón (Evita) — beloved First Lady who championed workers' rights
inventions: Ballpoint Pen (László Bíró, patented in Argentina)
Animated Films (Quirino Cristiani made the first animated feature)
Fingerprint Identification System (Juan Vucetich)
worldRecords: Aconcagua is the tallest mountain outside of Asia at 22,837 feet
Avenida 9 de Julio in Buenos Aires is one of the widest avenues in the world
weirdLaws: In Argentina, there was once a law requiring that tango be played in a certain percentage of radio music
uniqueAnimals: Patagonian Mara — a large rodent that looks like a mix between a rabbit and a deer
Andean Condor — one of the largest flying birds in the world with a 10-foot wingspan
sportsAchievements: Argentina won the FIFA World Cup in 1978, 1986, and 2022
Lionel Messi has won 8 Ballon d'Or awards — more than any player in history
history: Argentina declared independence from Spain on July 9, 1816.
The country attracted millions of immigrants from Europe in the late 1800s and early 1900s.
Eva Perón became a national icon in the 1940s–50s fighting for the poor and working class.
Argentina and the UK fought the Falklands War in 1982 over islands in the South Atlantic.
trivia: Argentina is named after 'argentum,' the Latin word for silver, because explorers believed it was full of silver.
Buenos Aires has more bookstores per person than any other city in the world.
Dinosaur fossils of some of the largest species ever found were discovered in Patagonia, Argentina.

southkorea.name: South Korea
southkorea.flagDescription: A white background with a red and blue yin-yang symbol (Taeguk) in the center, surrounded by four black trigrams in each corner.
southkorea.famousFor: K-pop music, Korean BBQ, cutting-edge technology (Samsung, LG), and the global hit show Squid Game.
landmarks: DMZ (Demilitarized Zone between North and South Korea)

geography.longestRiver: Nakdong River — 326 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Hallasan — 6,398 feet (on Jeju Island)

famousFood: Kimchi — fermented spicy cabbage served with almost every meal
Korean BBQ — grilling marinated meats right at your table
Bibimbap — a colorful rice bowl topped with vegetables, meat, egg, and spicy sauce
festivals: Boryeong Mud Festival — a fun festival where people play in mud, attracting visitors from around the world
famousPeople: BTS — the biggest K-pop group in history, breaking music records worldwide
Bong Joon-ho — director of Parasite, the first non-English film to win Best Picture at the Oscars
Yuna Kim — Olympic gold medal figure skater and national hero
inventions: MP3 Player (developed by Samsung)
Ondol (underfloor heating system used for centuries)
Metal Movable Type (invented before Gutenberg)
worldRecords: South Korea has the fastest average internet speed in the world
Seoul's Gangnam District inspired the most-viewed YouTube video of all time (Gangnam Style — 5+ billion views)
weirdLaws: In South Korea, online gaming for children under 16 was restricted between midnight and 6 AM (the 'Cinderella Law')
uniqueAnimals: Korean Water Deer — a small deer with tiny fangs instead of antlers
Asiatic Black Bear — a bear with a distinctive white V-shape on its chest, living in Korean mountains
sportsAchievements: South Korea co-hosted the 2002 FIFA World Cup and reached the semifinals — a first for any Asian team
Taekwondo, invented in Korea, became an official Olympic sport in 2000
history: Korea has over 5,000 years of history, with ancient kingdoms like Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla.
Korea was under Japanese rule from 1910 to 1945, and the Korean language was nearly banned during this time.
After World War II, Korea was divided into North and South, leading to the Korean War (1950–1953).
South Korea transformed from one of the poorest countries to a tech superpower in just a few decades — called the 'Miracle on the Han River.'
trivia: South Koreans celebrate their birthday by eating seaweed soup — it's a tradition!
The Korean alphabet (Hangul) was invented by King Sejong in 1443 and is considered one of the most scientific writing systems ever created.
South Korea has a professional esports (video game) league that fills stadiums with fans.

turkey.name: Turkey
turkey.flagDescription: A red background with a white crescent moon and a white star in the center.
turkey.famousFor: Bridging Europe and Asia, the ancient city of Istanbul, and being home to some of the oldest civilizations in the world.
landmarks: Cappadocia's fairy chimneys and hot air balloons

geography.longestRiver: Kızılırmak — 842 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Ararat — 16,854 feet

famousFood: Kebab — grilled meat on a skewer, famous worldwide in many styles
Baklava — layers of flaky pastry filled with nuts and soaked in honey syrup
Turkish Delight (Lokum) — soft, chewy candy dusted with powdered sugar
festivals: International Istanbul Film Festival — one of the most prestigious film festivals in the region
famousPeople: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk — founder of modern Turkey and its first president
Rumi — one of the world's most beloved poets, who lived in Turkey in the 1200s
Sinan — master architect of the Ottoman Empire who designed over 300 buildings
inventions: Turkish Bath (Hammam)
Yogurt (developed by Turkic peoples)
Tulip cultivation (brought tulips to Europe before the Netherlands made them famous)
worldRecords: Istanbul is the only city in the world that sits on two continents — Europe and Asia
Turkey's Grand Bazaar in Istanbul is one of the oldest and largest covered markets in the world with over 4,000 shops
weirdLaws: During the Ottoman Empire, if a husband did not provide his wife with coffee, it was grounds for divorce
uniqueAnimals: Van Cat — a rare all-white cat from eastern Turkey known for having one blue eye and one amber eye
Turkish Angora — one of the oldest domesticated cat breeds in the world
sportsAchievements: Turkey's national oil wrestling championship (Kırkpınar) has been held since 1362 — the longest-running sporting competition in the world
Turkey has won multiple European basketball championships
history: Istanbul (formerly Constantinople) was the capital of the Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman Empires.
The Ottoman Empire lasted over 600 years and controlled much of Southeast Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa.
Ancient Troy, the city from Homer's Iliad, was located in what is now Turkey.
Modern Turkey was founded in 1923 by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk after the fall of the Ottoman Empire.
trivia: Turkey introduced coffee to Europe — the word 'coffee' comes from the Turkish word 'kahve.'
Santa Claus is based on Saint Nicholas, who was born in Patara, Turkey, around 270 AD.
Cappadocia's underground cities could shelter up to 20,000 people and were used to hide from invaders.

southafrica.name: South Africa
southafrica.language: 11 official languages including Zulu, Xhosa, Afrikaans, and English
southafrica.flagDescription: A colorful flag with a sideways Y-shape in green, bordered by white and gold, on a background of red, blue, and black.
southafrica.famousFor: Nelson Mandela's fight for freedom, incredible wildlife safaris, and having three capital cities.

geography.longestRiver: Orange River — 1,367 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mafadi — 11,322 feet

famousFood: Biltong — dried and cured meat similar to jerky but much more flavorful
Bobotie — a spiced minced meat dish topped with a baked egg custard
Braai — South Africa's beloved barbecue tradition, a way of life for many
festivals: Cape Town Carnival — a vibrant street parade with music, dance, and costumes celebrating South Africa's diverse cultures
famousPeople: Nelson Mandela — anti-apartheid leader who spent 27 years in prison and became South Africa's first Black president
Desmond Tutu — Nobel Peace Prize winner who fought against apartheid
Elon Musk — tech entrepreneur (Tesla, SpaceX) born in Pretoria, South Africa
inventions: CAT Scan (CT Scanner)
Dolosse (concrete blocks used to protect harbors)
Kreepy Krauly (automatic pool cleaner)
worldRecords: South Africa is the only country in the world with three capital cities
The Blyde River Canyon is one of the largest canyons on Earth and the largest green canyon
weirdLaws: In South Africa, it is technically illegal to wrestle with a lion — yes, someone needed to make that a law
uniqueAnimals: African Penguin — the only penguin species that breeds in Africa, found along the coast
Springbok — a fast antelope that can leap 13 feet in the air, also the national animal
Pangolin — the world's most trafficked mammal, a scaly ant-eating animal found in South Africa
sportsAchievements: South Africa's rugby team (the Springboks) won the Rugby World Cup in 1995, 2007, 2019, and 2023
South Africa hosted the 2010 FIFA World Cup — the first African nation to do so
history: South Africa was ruled by apartheid, a system of racial segregation, from 1948 to 1994.
Nelson Mandela was released from prison in 1990 and became president in 1994 in the country's first free elections.
The Zulu Kingdom was one of the most powerful African empires, led by the great warrior king Shaka Zulu in the early 1800s.
Gold was discovered near Johannesburg in 1886, sparking a gold rush that transformed the region.
trivia: South Africa is called the Rainbow Nation because of its many diverse cultures and languages.
The oldest known fossils of early humans were found in South Africa — it's called the Cradle of Humankind.
South Africa is the world's largest producer of platinum.

nigeria.name: Nigeria
nigeria.language: English (official), with Hausa, Yoruba, and Igbo widely spoken
nigeria.flagDescription: Three vertical stripes — green on the left, white in the middle, and green on the right. The green represents forests and agriculture.
nigeria.famousFor: Being the most populous country in Africa, its booming movie industry (Nollywood), and rich cultural diversity.
landmarks: Zuma Rock — a massive monolith near Abuja

geography.longestRiver: Niger River — 2,600 miles (total length)
geography.tallestPeak: Chappal Waddi — 7,936 feet

famousFood: Jollof Rice — a beloved one-pot rice dish cooked in tomato sauce with spices
Suya — spicy grilled meat skewers sold as popular street food
Pounded Yam and Egusi Soup — a hearty dish made from yam and melon seed soup
festivals: Lagos Carnival — a massive street party with music, dance, and colorful costumes, sometimes called Africa's Mardi Gras
famousPeople: Wole Soyinka — the first African to win the Nobel Prize in Literature
Wizkid — globally famous Afrobeats musician who has collaborated with top artists worldwide
Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie — celebrated author and speaker known for her TED Talk on feminism
inventions: Nollywood (the second-largest film industry in the world by number of movies produced)
Afrobeats music genre that has taken over global charts
Cashless payment innovations in African mobile banking
worldRecords: Nigeria is the most populous country in Africa with over 224 million people
Nollywood produces about 2,500 films per year, making it the second-largest movie industry by volume
weirdLaws: In Lagos, motorcycles (okadas) have been banned from many major roads for safety reasons
uniqueAnimals: Drill Monkey — one of Africa's most endangered primates, found in Nigeria's forests
Nigerian-Cameroon Chimpanzee — a rare subspecies of chimpanzee found in the border region
sportsAchievements: Nigeria's Super Eagles have won the Africa Cup of Nations three times
Nigeria won the Olympic gold medal in men's soccer in 1996 — the first African team to do so
history: The ancient Nok civilization in Nigeria created stunning terracotta sculptures over 2,500 years ago.
The Benin Kingdom (in modern-day Nigeria) was famous for its incredible bronze sculptures and powerful rulers.
Nigeria gained independence from Britain on October 1, 1960.
Nigeria experienced a civil war (the Biafran War) from 1967 to 1970.
trivia: Nigeria has over 250 ethnic groups and more than 500 languages spoken across the country.
Lagos is one of the fastest-growing cities in the world and could become the world's largest city by 2100.
The Jollof Rice rivalry between Nigeria and Ghana is one of the funniest and most passionate food debates in Africa.

kenya.name: Kenya
kenya.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes — black on top, red in the middle, and green on the bottom, separated by white edges, with a Maasai shield and spears in the center.
kenya.famousFor: World-famous safari parks, long-distance runners who dominate global marathons, and being the home of the Great Migration.
landmarks: Lamu Old Town (a UNESCO World Heritage Site)

geography.longestRiver: Tana River — 621 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Kenya (Batian Peak) — 17,057 feet

famousFood: Nyama Choma — grilled meat (usually goat or beef) that's Kenya's national dish
Ugali — a thick cornmeal porridge eaten with stews and vegetables
Mandazi — a puffy, slightly sweet fried bread eaten as a snack or breakfast
famousPeople: Eliud Kipchoge — the greatest marathon runner ever, first person to run a marathon in under 2 hours
Wangari Maathai — first African woman to win the Nobel Peace Prize for environmental work
Lupita Nyong'o — Oscar-winning actress born in Mexico and raised in Kenya
inventions: M-Pesa (revolutionary mobile money system that changed banking in Africa)
Flip-flop recycling art (turning ocean plastic into colorful art)
Use of geothermal energy from the Great Rift Valley
worldRecords: Kenya holds more marathon world records than any other country
The Great Wildebeest Migration in Kenya's Maasai Mara involves over 1.5 million animals
weirdLaws: It is illegal to destroy or burn Kenyan currency — even if it's your own money
uniqueAnimals: Big Five — Kenya is home to lions, leopards, elephants, rhinos, and buffalo
Grevy's Zebra — the largest and most endangered zebra species, found mainly in Kenya
Flamingos of Lake Nakuru — millions of pink flamingos gather at this Kenyan lake
sportsAchievements: Kenyan runners have won more Olympic medals in long-distance running than any other country
Eliud Kipchoge broke the 2-hour marathon barrier in 2019 with a time of 1:59:40
history: Some of the oldest human fossils ever found were discovered near Lake Turkana in Kenya, dating back millions of years.
Kenya was a British colony from the late 1800s until it gained independence on December 12, 1963.
Jomo Kenyatta became Kenya's first president, and the country's capital Nairobi grew from a railway camp to a major city.
The Mau Mau uprising in the 1950s was a key independence movement against British colonial rule.
trivia: Kenya sits right on the equator, meaning it has almost exactly 12 hours of daylight every day of the year.
Nairobi is one of the only cities in the world with a national park inside its borders — you can see giraffes with skyscrapers in the background.
The name 'Kenya' comes from Mount Kenya, which means 'Mountain of Whiteness' in the local Kamba language.

newzealand.name: New Zealand
newzealand.language: English, Māori, and New Zealand Sign Language
newzealand.flagDescription: A dark blue background with the Union Jack in the upper left corner and four red stars with white borders representing the Southern Cross constellation.
newzealand.famousFor: Breathtaking scenery used as the filming location for The Lord of the Rings, and the Māori haka dance.

geography.longestRiver: Waikato River — 264 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Aoraki / Mount Cook — 12,218 feet

famousFood: Pavlova — a crispy meringue dessert topped with whipped cream and fresh fruit
Hangi — a traditional Māori feast cooked underground using hot stones
Meat Pie — a savory pastry filled with minced meat and gravy, a classic Kiwi snack
famousPeople: Sir Edmund Hillary — first person (with Tenzing Norgay) to reach the summit of Mount Everest in 1953
Peter Jackson — director of The Lord of the Rings trilogy
Ernest Rutherford — Nobel Prize-winning physicist who split the atom
inventions: Bungee Jumping (commercial)
Jet Boat (Sir William Hamilton)
Zorbing (rolling downhill in a giant inflatable ball)
worldRecords: New Zealand was the first country to give women the right to vote in 1893
New Zealand has more sheep than people — about 5 sheep for every 1 person
weirdLaws: It is illegal to fly a kite in a public place in a way that annoys other people
uniqueAnimals: Kiwi — a small, flightless bird with whisker-like feathers and nostrils at the tip of its beak
Tuatara — a reptile that looks like a lizard but belongs to a group that lived alongside dinosaurs over 200 million years ago
Hector's Dolphin — the smallest and rarest dolphin in the world, found only in New Zealand
sportsAchievements: The All Blacks rugby team is one of the most successful sports teams in history, with over a 77% win rate
New Zealand has won the Rugby World Cup three times (1987, 2011, 2015)
history: Māori people arrived in New Zealand around 1250–1300 AD after voyaging across the Pacific Ocean in canoes.
New Zealand was one of the last large landmasses to be settled by humans.
The Treaty of Waitangi was signed in 1840 between the British Crown and Māori chiefs — it remains hugely important today.
New Zealand became the first country to grant all women the right to vote in 1893.
trivia: New Zealanders are called Kiwis, named after their national bird — not the fruit!
There are no native land snakes in New Zealand — it's one of the few snake-free countries.
The Lord of the Rings movies were entirely filmed in New Zealand, and you can visit Hobbiton in Matamata.

norway.name: Norway
norway.flagDescription: A red background with a blue cross outlined in white, offset to the left side.
norway.famousFor: Stunning fjords, the Northern Lights (Aurora Borealis), and Vikings.
landmarks: The Viking Ship Museum in Oslo

geography.longestRiver: Glomma River — 380 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Galdhøpiggen — 8,100 feet

famousFood: Brunost — a sweet brown cheese unique to Norway, made from caramelized whey
Rakfisk — fermented trout, a traditional Norwegian delicacy
Smoked Salmon (Laks) — Norway is one of the world's biggest producers of salmon
festivals: Syttende Mai (May 17th) — Norway's Constitution Day, celebrated with parades, traditional costumes (bunad), and lots of ice cream
famousPeople: Roald Amundsen — the first person to reach the South Pole in 1911
Edvard Munch — painter of The Scream, one of the most famous artworks in the world
Edvard Grieg — composer famous for 'In the Hall of the Mountain King'
inventions: Cheese Slicer (Ostehøvel)
Paper Clip (Johan Vaaler)
Spray Can (Erik Rotheim)
worldRecords: Norway has won more Winter Olympic medals than any other country
The world's longest road tunnel is in Norway — the Lærdal Tunnel at 15 miles long
weirdLaws: In Svalbard (a Norwegian island territory), it is technically required to carry a rifle outside settlements to protect against polar bears
uniqueAnimals: Norwegian Lemming — a small, colorful rodent famous for mass migrations
Arctic Fox — a fluffy white fox that survives Norway's freezing Arctic winters
Atlantic Puffin — a colorful seabird with an orange beak, found along Norway's coast
sportsAchievements: Norway has won more Winter Olympic gold medals than any other nation (over 140)
Norwegian cross-country skiers dominate the sport — Marit Bjørgen won 15 Olympic medals
history: The Vikings came from Norway (and Scandinavia) and explored, traded, and raided across Europe from the 700s to the 1000s AD.
Vikings from Norway were the first Europeans to reach North America — about 500 years before Columbus.
Norway was united with Denmark for over 400 years and then with Sweden until gaining full independence in 1905.
During World War II, Norway was occupied by Nazi Germany for five years (1940–1945).
trivia: In northern Norway, the sun never sets for part of the summer (Midnight Sun) and never rises for part of the winter (Polar Night).
Norway gives a giant Christmas tree to London every year as a thank-you gift for British support during World War II.
Norway has more than 1,000 fjords — deep, narrow waterways carved by glaciers.

thailand.name: Thailand
thailand.flagDescription: Five horizontal stripes: red, white, blue (double width), white, and red — the blue represents the monarchy
thailand.famousFor: Ornate temples, tropical beaches, and incredible street food
landmarks: Wat Pho (Temple of the Reclining Buddha)

geography.longestRiver: Mekong River — 2,703 miles, forms much of Thailand's northeastern border
geography.tallestPeak: Doi Inthanon — 8,415 feet, the highest point in Thailand

famousFood: Pad Thai — stir-fried rice noodles with shrimp, peanuts, and lime
Tom Yum Goong — spicy and sour shrimp soup with lemongrass
Mango Sticky Rice — sweet coconut rice topped with fresh mango slices
festivals: Songkran — Thai New Year celebrated with the world's biggest water fight every April
Loy Krathong — Festival of Lights where people float decorated baskets on rivers
famousPeople: King Bhumibol Adulyadej — the world's longest-reigning monarch, ruled for 70 years
Tony Jaa — martial arts movie star famous for Muay Thai stunts
Apichatpong Weerasethakul — won the top prize at the Cannes Film Festival
inventions: Red Bull energy drink (originally called Krating Daeng)
Tuk-tuks (three-wheeled motorized taxis)
Thai massage techniques developed over 2,500 years
worldRecords: Bangkok's full ceremonial name is 168 letters long — the longest city name in the world
Thailand has the world's largest solid gold Buddha statue, weighing 5.5 tons
weirdLaws: It is illegal to step on Thai money because it has the King's image on it
uniqueAnimals: Siamese Cat — one of the world's oldest and most popular cat breeds, originally from Thailand
Asian Elephant — Thailand's national symbol and a sacred animal
Tokay Gecko — a large, brightly colored gecko with a loud call
sportsAchievements: Thailand has won more Olympic medals in boxing and weightlifting than any other Southeast Asian country
history: Thailand is the only Southeast Asian country never colonized by a European power.
The ancient kingdom of Ayutthaya was one of the largest cities in the world in the 1700s.
Thailand was known as Siam until 1939 — that's where Siamese cats get their name!
Thai kings built over 40,000 temples across the country over the centuries.
trivia: The full name of Bangkok in Thai has 168 letters and is the longest place name in the world.
Thailand is the world's largest exporter of rice.
Monkeys in the city of Lopburi have their own annual buffet feast thrown by locals.

vietnam.name: Vietnam
vietnam.flagDescription: A bright red background with a large yellow five-pointed star in the center
vietnam.famousFor: Stunning natural landscapes, delicious pho noodle soup, and rich ancient history
landmarks: Ha Long Bay — thousands of limestone islands rising from the sea
Cu Chi Tunnels — a massive underground tunnel network
Hoi An Ancient Town — a beautifully preserved trading port

geography.longestRiver: Mekong River — 2,703 miles, forms a massive delta in southern Vietnam
geography.tallestPeak: Fansipan — 10,326 feet, called the Roof of Indochina

famousFood: Pho — a fragrant noodle soup with beef or chicken, herbs, and broth simmered for hours
Banh Mi — a crispy baguette sandwich filled with meats, pickled veggies, and herbs
Spring Rolls — fresh or fried rolls stuffed with shrimp, pork, and vegetables
festivals: Tet (Lunar New Year) — Vietnam's biggest celebration with fireworks, family feasts, and lucky red envelopes
famousPeople: Ho Chi Minh — founder of modern Vietnam and its most famous leader
Nguyen Du — celebrated poet who wrote The Tale of Kieu
Nguyen Hue — legendary emperor who defeated a Chinese invasion in 1789
inventions: Vietnamese coffee with condensed milk (ca phe sua da)
Water puppetry — a 1,000-year-old art form performed on water
Ao dai — the elegant traditional Vietnamese outfit
worldRecords: Son Doong Cave in Vietnam is the world's largest cave — big enough to fit a 40-story building inside
Vietnam is the world's second-largest coffee exporter
weirdLaws: In Vietnam, it's technically illegal to play video games at internet cafes after 10 PM
uniqueAnimals: Saola — an extremely rare forest-dwelling antelope discovered only in 1992
Tonkin Snub-Nosed Monkey — one of the rarest primates on Earth
Red-Shanked Douc Langur — often called the most colorful monkey in the world
sportsAchievements: Vietnam won the 2018 AFF Suzuki Cup in soccer, uniting the entire country in celebration
history: Vietnam has over 4,000 years of history and was ruled by China for about 1,000 years.
The Vietnamese defeated the powerful Mongol Empire three times in the 1200s.
Ha Long Bay means 'Descending Dragon' — legend says a dragon carved the bay with its tail.
Vietnam reunified as one country in 1975 after decades of war.
trivia: Vietnam has more motorbikes than cars — there are over 45 million motorbikes on the roads!
Son Doong Cave is so big it has its own weather system with clouds forming inside.
Vietnamese is a tonal language — the same word can mean six different things depending on tone.

indonesia.name: Indonesia
indonesia.flagDescription: Two horizontal stripes: red on top and white on the bottom — one of the simplest flags in the world
indonesia.famousFor: Being the world's largest island country with over 17,000 islands
landmarks: Borobudur Temple — the world's largest Buddhist temple
Komodo National Park — home of the Komodo dragon
Bali's Tanah Lot Temple — a sea temple perched on a rock

geography.longestRiver: Kapuas River — 710 miles, the longest river on the island of Borneo
geography.tallestPeak: Puncak Jaya — 16,024 feet, the tallest peak between the Himalayas and the Andes

famousFood: Nasi Goreng — Indonesian fried rice with sweet soy sauce, egg, and shrimp crackers
Satay — grilled meat skewers served with peanut sauce
Rendang — a slow-cooked spicy beef stew voted the world's most delicious food
festivals: Nyepi (Day of Silence) — Bali's New Year when the entire island shuts down for 24 hours, even the airport
famousPeople: Sukarno — Indonesia's founding president who united the nation
Susi Susanti — Olympic gold medalist in badminton
Pramoedya Ananta Toer — Indonesia's most famous novelist
inventions: Batik fabric dyeing — an ancient art form recognized by UNESCO
Gamelan music — an orchestra of metallic percussion instruments
Jamu — traditional herbal medicine made from roots and spices
worldRecords: Indonesia has the most islands of any country — over 17,000!
It has the world's largest flower, the Rafflesia, which can grow 3 feet wide and smells like rotting meat
weirdLaws: In Indonesia, the penalty for cutting down a protected tree can be harsher than for some other crimes
uniqueAnimals: Komodo Dragon — the world's largest living lizard, up to 10 feet long
Orangutan — one of our closest relatives, found only in Borneo and Sumatra
Javan Rhino — one of the rarest mammals on Earth with fewer than 80 left
sportsAchievements: Indonesia has won 8 Olympic gold medals in badminton — more than any other country
Indonesia's badminton team has won the Thomas Cup 14 times
history: Indonesia was home to 'Java Man,' one of the earliest known human ancestors, about 1 million years old.
The ancient Majapahit Empire ruled most of Southeast Asia in the 1300s.
Indonesia declared independence from the Netherlands in 1945.
The 1883 eruption of Krakatoa in Indonesia was one of the loudest sounds in recorded history — heard 3,000 miles away!
trivia: Indonesia spans three time zones and is wider than the entire United States!
There are over 700 languages spoken across Indonesia's islands.
Rendang, a spicy beef dish from Indonesia, was voted the world's most delicious food by CNN.

saudiarabia.name: Saudi Arabia
saudiarabia.flagDescription: A green background with a white Arabic inscription (the shahada) and a white sword beneath it
saudiarabia.famousFor: Being home to Islam's two holiest cities: Mecca and Medina
landmarks: Masjid al-Haram (Great Mosque of Mecca) — the largest mosque in the world
Madain Saleh — ancient Nabataean rock-carved tombs
The Kingdom Centre Tower in Riyadh

geography.longestRiver: Saudi Arabia has no permanent rivers — it is one of the largest countries without one!
geography.tallestPeak: Jabal Sawda — 9,843 feet, located in the Asir Mountains near Yemen

famousFood: Kabsa — spiced rice with meat, the national dish
Dates — Saudi Arabia grows over 400 varieties of dates
Shawarma — roasted meat wrapped in flatbread with garlic sauce
festivals: Hajj — the world's largest annual gathering, where millions of Muslims from around the world make a pilgrimage to Mecca
famousPeople: King Abdulaziz — founder of modern Saudi Arabia in 1932
Haifa al-Mansour — Saudi Arabia's first female film director
Ibn Battuta-era scholars who helped spread knowledge across the Islamic world
inventions: Arabic numerals (0-9) — the number system the whole world uses today
Algebra — the word 'algebra' comes from Arabic
Astrolabe improvements — early GPS-like navigation tools used by Arab astronomers
worldRecords: Saudi Arabia has the largest continuous sand desert in the world — the Rub' al Khali (Empty Quarter)
The Masjid al-Haram in Mecca can hold up to 4 million worshippers
weirdLaws: There are no movie theaters were allowed in Saudi Arabia until 2018 — the ban lasted 35 years!
uniqueAnimals: Arabian Oryx — a white antelope that was once extinct in the wild but was brought back through breeding programs
Sand Cat — a tiny wild cat adapted to survive in the harsh desert
sportsAchievements: Saudi Arabia stunned the world by beating Argentina 2-1 at the 2022 FIFA World Cup — one of the biggest upsets in soccer history
history: Saudi Arabia is home to Mecca and Medina, the two holiest cities in Islam.
The Arabian Peninsula was a major crossroads for trade routes connecting Asia, Africa, and Europe.
Modern Saudi Arabia was founded in 1932 by King Abdulaziz (Ibn Saud).
Oil was discovered in Saudi Arabia in 1938, transforming it into one of the wealthiest nations on Earth.
trivia: Saudi Arabia's Rub' al Khali desert is larger than France and has sand dunes over 800 feet tall.
Women were allowed to drive in Saudi Arabia starting in 2018.
The country has zero permanent rivers or lakes — all water comes from underground or desalination plants.

israel.name: Israel
israel.flagDescription: A white background with two horizontal blue stripes and a blue Star of David in the center
israel.famousFor: Ancient holy sites sacred to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, and cutting-edge technology
landmarks: Western Wall — the holiest site in Judaism
Dead Sea — the lowest point on Earth
Masada — an ancient fortress on a cliff in the desert

geography.longestRiver: Jordan River — 156 miles, one of the most historically important rivers in the world
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Hermon — 7,336 feet, the only place in Israel where you can ski

famousFood: Falafel — crispy fried chickpea balls served in pita with tahini
Hummus — creamy chickpea dip that Israelis eat with almost everything
Shakshuka — eggs poached in spicy tomato sauce, a popular breakfast dish
festivals: Hanukkah — the Festival of Lights celebrating an ancient miracle with candles and fried foods
famousPeople: Albert Einstein — offered the presidency of Israel (he declined!)
Gal Gadot — actress who played Wonder Woman
Golda Meir — one of the world's first female prime ministers
inventions: USB flash drive — invented by an Israeli company
Iron Dome — a missile defense system that intercepts rockets mid-air
Cherry tomatoes — developed by Israeli scientists for better flavor
worldRecords: The Dead Sea is the lowest point on Earth at 1,412 feet below sea level
Israel has more startups per person than any other country in the world
weirdLaws: It is illegal to bring bears to the beach in Israel (yes, really!)
uniqueAnimals: Israeli Gazelle — a graceful gazelle found only in Israel
Hyrax — a small furry animal that looks like a guinea pig but is actually related to elephants
sportsAchievements: Israel's Maccabi Tel Aviv basketball team has won 6 European championships
history: Jerusalem is over 3,000 years old and is considered holy by Jews, Christians, and Muslims.
The Dead Sea Scrolls, some of the oldest known biblical texts, were discovered in caves near the Dead Sea in 1947.
Modern Israel was established as a country in 1948.
Ancient Israel was home to King David and King Solomon, who built the First Temple in Jerusalem.
trivia: You can float in the Dead Sea without trying because the water is nearly 10 times saltier than the ocean.
Israel is about the size of New Jersey but has deserts, mountains, beaches, and snow.
Hebrew was a 'dead' language for nearly 2,000 years before being revived as a spoken language.

philippines.name: Philippines
philippines.flagDescription: Two horizontal stripes — blue on top and red on bottom — with a white triangle on the left containing a sun and three stars
philippines.famousFor: Over 7,000 beautiful tropical islands, friendly people, and incredible biodiversity
landmarks: Chocolate Hills — over 1,200 perfectly cone-shaped hills in Bohol
Banaue Rice Terraces — 2,000-year-old terraces carved into mountains
Puerto Princesa Underground River — one of the longest underground rivers in the world

geography.longestRiver: Cagayan River — 314 miles, the longest river in the Philippines
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Apo — 9,692 feet, the highest peak in the Philippines

famousFood: Adobo — chicken or pork braised in soy sauce, vinegar, and garlic
Lumpia — crispy spring rolls filled with meat and vegetables
Halo-Halo — a colorful shaved ice dessert with sweet beans, fruit, and ice cream
festivals: Sinulog Festival — a giant street dance festival in Cebu with colorful costumes and music
Ati-Atihan — one of the oldest festivals in the Philippines, known as the 'Mother of All Festivals'
famousPeople: Jose Rizal — national hero whose writings inspired Philippine independence
Manny Pacquiao — one of the greatest boxers of all time, won titles in 8 weight classes
Lea Salonga — Broadway star and the singing voice of Disney's Jasmine and Mulan
inventions: Karaoke — the modern sing-along machine was invented by Filipino Roberto del Rosario
Banana ketchup — invented during WWII when tomatoes were scarce
Salt and pepper shaker with a single container — a Filipino innovation
worldRecords: The Philippines has the longest Christmas season in the world — starting in September!
Manny Pacquiao is the only boxer to win world titles in 8 different weight divisions
weirdLaws: In the Philippines, it is illegal to sing the national anthem incorrectly — you can be fined!
uniqueAnimals: Philippine Eagle — the world's largest eagle by wing surface area, found nowhere else on Earth
Tarsier — one of the world's smallest primates with enormous eyes, each eyeball as big as its brain
Philippine Mouse Deer — one of the tiniest hoofed animals in the world
sportsAchievements: Manny Pacquiao is the only boxer in history to win world titles in 8 different weight classes
Hidilyn Diaz won the Philippines' first-ever Olympic gold medal in weightlifting in 2021
history: The Philippines was named after King Philip II of Spain in the 1500s.
The islands were a Spanish colony for over 300 years before gaining independence.
Jose Rizal, a doctor and writer, inspired a revolution through his novels about colonial injustice.
The Philippines was the first country in Asia to gain independence from a European power in 1898.
trivia: Filipinos start celebrating Christmas in September — that's four months of holiday cheer!
The Philippines has more than 7,600 islands, but only about 2,000 are inhabited.
Jeepneys — colorful converted military jeeps — are the most popular form of public transport.

pakistan.name: Pakistan
pakistan.flagDescription: A dark green background with a white vertical stripe on the left, and a white crescent moon and five-pointed star
pakistan.nationalAnimal: Markhor (a wild goat with twisted horns)
pakistan.famousFor: The mighty Karakoram mountain range, ancient Indus Valley civilizations, and passionate cricket fans
landmarks: Badshahi Mosque — one of the largest mosques in the world
Mohenjo-daro — ruins of a 4,500-year-old city from the Indus Valley Civilization

geography.longestRiver: Indus River — 1,988 miles, the lifeline of Pakistan and one of the world's great rivers
geography.tallestPeak: K2 — 28,251 feet, the second-highest mountain in the world and harder to climb than Everest

famousFood: Biryani — fragrant rice layered with spiced meat, a beloved dish across Pakistan
Nihari — a slow-cooked spicy beef stew eaten for breakfast
Chapli Kebab — spiced minced meat patties from the Pashtun region
festivals: Basant (Kite Festival) — a colorful spring festival where the sky fills with thousands of kites
famousPeople: Malala Yousafzai — youngest-ever Nobel Peace Prize winner, advocate for girls' education
Imran Khan — cricket legend who led Pakistan to a World Cup win
Abdus Salam — first Pakistani Nobel Prize winner in Physics
inventions: Contributions to algebra and astronomy by ancient scholars of the region
Polo — believed to have originated in the region that is now northern Pakistan
The surgical techniques of early Indus Valley civilization
worldRecords: K2 is considered the most dangerous mountain to climb — only about 400 people have ever summited
The Karakoram Highway is the highest paved international road in the world at 15,397 feet
weirdLaws: In Pakistan, you are not allowed to modify your car exhaust to make it louder — but many people still do!
uniqueAnimals: Markhor — a majestic wild goat with corkscrew horns, Pakistan's national animal
Indus River Dolphin — a rare freshwater dolphin that is nearly blind
Snow Leopard — the elusive 'ghost of the mountains' found in Pakistan's high peaks
sportsAchievements: Pakistan has won the Cricket World Cup (1992), Champions Trophy, and World T20
Pakistan has won 4 Olympic gold medals in field hockey
history: The Indus Valley Civilization, one of the world's oldest, flourished in Pakistan's region over 4,500 years ago.
Mohenjo-daro had advanced drainage systems and city planning thousands of years before modern cities.
Pakistan became an independent country in 1947 when British India was divided.
The ancient Gandhara region of Pakistan was a major center of Buddhist art and learning.
trivia: K2 is called the 'Savage Mountain' because about 1 in 4 climbers who attempt it don't survive.
Pakistan's truck art is world-famous — trucks are decorated with colorful paintings and lights.
Cricket isn't just a sport in Pakistan — it's practically a religion!

singapore.name: Singapore
singapore.flagDescription: Two horizontal halves — red on top and white on bottom — with a white crescent moon and five stars in the upper left
singapore.famousFor: Being an ultra-modern, super clean city-state with amazing food from many cultures
landmarks: Marina Bay Sands — a hotel with a rooftop infinity pool shaped like a surfboard
Gardens by the Bay — futuristic 'supertree' structures up to 16 stories tall
Merlion Statue — a half-lion, half-fish statue and Singapore's icon

geography.longestRiver: Kallang River — only 6 miles long, showing just how tiny Singapore is!
geography.tallestPeak: Bukit Timah Hill — only 538 feet, one of the shortest 'tallest peaks' of any country

famousFood: Hainanese Chicken Rice — perfectly poached chicken with fragrant rice, a national obsession
Chili Crab — stir-fried crab in sweet and spicy tomato-chili sauce
Laksa — a spicy coconut curry noodle soup blending Chinese and Malay flavors
festivals: Chinese New Year — celebrated with lion dances, fireworks, and red envelopes across Singapore
famousPeople: Lee Kuan Yew — founding father who transformed Singapore from a tiny port into a global powerhouse
Joseph Schooling — beat Michael Phelps to win Singapore's first Olympic gold medal
Fandi Ahmad — Singapore's most legendary soccer player
inventions: The thumb drive (USB flash drive) — partly developed by Singapore's Trek Technology
Tiger Balm — the famous pain-relief ointment
NEWater — ultra-clean recycled drinking water technology
worldRecords: Singapore's Changi Airport has been voted the world's best airport for over 10 years straight
Singapore is one of only three surviving city-states in the world (along with Monaco and Vatican City)
weirdLaws: Chewing gum has been banned in Singapore since 1992 — you can't buy or sell it!
uniqueAnimals: Oriental Pied Hornbill — a striking black-and-white bird that has returned to Singapore's forests
Smooth-Coated Otter — families of otters have become Singapore's beloved urban wildlife celebrities
sportsAchievements: Joseph Schooling won the 100m butterfly gold medal at the 2016 Olympics, beating Michael Phelps
history: Singapore's name comes from 'Singapura,' meaning 'Lion City' in Sanskrit.
It was a British colony and major trading port before gaining independence in 1965.
In just 50 years, Singapore went from a developing nation to one of the richest countries in the world.
During World War II, the fall of Singapore to Japan in 1942 was one of Britain's worst military defeats.
trivia: Singapore is so small you can drive across the entire country in about 45 minutes.
Chewing gum is banned — even bringing it into the country can get you in trouble!
Despite its tiny size, Singapore has 4 official languages: English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil.

malaysia.name: Malaysia
malaysia.flagDescription: Fourteen red and white horizontal stripes with a blue rectangle containing a yellow crescent and 14-pointed star
malaysia.famousFor: The iconic Petronas Twin Towers, lush rainforests, and a delicious mix of Malay, Chinese, and Indian cuisines
landmarks: Petronas Twin Towers — once the tallest buildings in the world
Batu Caves — limestone caves with a giant golden Hindu statue
Mount Kinabalu — the tallest peak in Southeast Asia

geography.longestRiver: Rajang River — 350 miles, the longest river in Malaysia, flowing through Borneo's jungles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Kinabalu — 13,435 feet, the tallest mountain in Southeast Asia

famousFood: Nasi Lemak — coconut rice with sambal, anchovies, and peanuts, Malaysia's national dish
Roti Canai — flaky flatbread served with curry sauce
Char Kway Teow — smoky stir-fried flat noodles with shrimp and Chinese sausage
festivals: Thaipusam — a dramatic Hindu festival where devotees carry huge decorated structures called kavadis
famousPeople: Tunku Abdul Rahman — father of Malaysian independence
Jimmy Choo — world-famous luxury shoe designer
Nicol David — dominated women's squash as world number one for 9 years
inventions: Palm oil industry — Malaysia is one of the world's largest producers of palm oil
Modern rubber production — Malaysia pioneered commercial rubber plantations
Proton — Southeast Asia's first domestically produced car
worldRecords: The Petronas Twin Towers were the world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004
Malaysia's Sarawak Chamber is the world's largest underground cave chamber — big enough to hold 40 Boeing 747s
weirdLaws: In Malaysia, it is illegal to walk around your house without wearing clothes if your curtains are open
uniqueAnimals: Malayan Tiger — a critically endangered tiger found only in Peninsular Malaysia
Proboscis Monkey — a bizarre monkey with a huge droopy nose, found only in Borneo
Rafflesia — home to the world's largest flower, which smells like rotting flesh
sportsAchievements: Malaysia has won 8 Olympic silver medals, with badminton star Lee Chong Wei earning 3 of them
Malaysia is a powerhouse in badminton, consistently ranking among the world's best
history: The Malacca Sultanate in the 1400s made Malaysia a major center of trade and Islam in Southeast Asia.
Malaysia was colonized by the Portuguese, Dutch, and British before gaining independence in 1957.
The country is split into two parts — Peninsular Malaysia and East Malaysia on the island of Borneo.
Malaysia was formed in 1963 by uniting Malaya, Singapore, Sabah, and Sarawak (Singapore left in 1965).
trivia: Malaysia's rainforests are about 130 million years old — even older than the Amazon!
The country is home to the world's largest flower (Rafflesia) AND the world's largest cave chamber.
Kuala Lumpur means 'muddy confluence' because it was built where two muddy rivers meet.

iran.name: Iran
iran.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes — green, white, and red — with a red emblem in the center and stylized script along the stripe borders
iran.famousFor: The ancient Persian Empire, stunning mosques with intricate tilework, and being a cradle of civilization
landmarks: Persepolis — the magnificent ruins of the ancient Persian Empire's capital
Nasir al-Mulk Mosque — the stunning 'Pink Mosque' with rainbow stained-glass windows
Isfahan's Naqsh-e Jahan Square — one of the largest and most beautiful city squares in the world

geography.longestRiver: Karun River — 515 miles, Iran's only navigable river
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Damavand — 18,406 feet, the tallest volcano in Asia

famousFood: Chelo Kebab — saffron-flavored rice with juicy grilled meat, Iran's national dish
Ghormeh Sabzi — a savory herb stew considered Iran's most popular home-cooked meal
Tahdig — the beloved crispy golden rice crust from the bottom of the pot
festivals: Nowruz — Persian New Year, a 3,000-year-old spring celebration observed by over 300 million people worldwide
famousPeople: Cyrus the Great — founder of the Persian Empire and author of the first human rights declaration
Rumi — one of the most-read poets in the world, even 800 years later
Avicenna (Ibn Sina) — a medieval scholar whose medical textbook was used for 600 years
inventions: The postal system — the Persian Empire had the world's first organized mail delivery
Algebra — Persian mathematicians made key contributions to algebra
Windmills — Persia built some of the world's first windmills around 600 AD
Qanat irrigation system — underground water channels still used today
worldRecords: Iran has the world's largest collection of Persian carpets — carpet weaving has been an art there for 2,500 years
Nowruz is celebrated by over 300 million people, making it one of the world's oldest and most widely observed festivals
weirdLaws: In Iran, it is technically illegal for men to wear necklaces in public
uniqueAnimals: Asiatic Cheetah — fewer than 50 remain, found only in Iran
Persian Leopard — the largest leopard subspecies in the world
Persian Fallow Deer — once thought extinct, rediscovered in Iran in 1956
sportsAchievements: Iran is a wrestling powerhouse with over 50 Olympic and World Championship medals
Iran's national soccer team has qualified for 6 FIFA World Cups
history: The Persian Empire under Cyrus the Great was the largest empire the world had ever seen in 550 BC.
Cyrus the Great issued the Cyrus Cylinder, considered the world's first declaration of human rights.
Persia's Royal Road was 1,677 miles long — messages could travel its length in just 7 days!
Iran was called Persia by the Western world until 1935.
trivia: The word 'paradise' comes from the ancient Persian word 'pairidaeza,' meaning a walled garden.
Iran has more than 20 UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
Rumi, the world's best-selling poet in the United States, was Persian!

colombia.name: Colombia
colombia.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes: a wide yellow stripe on top, blue in the middle, and red on the bottom
colombia.nationalSport: Tejo (a sport involving throwing metal discs at gunpowder targets!)
colombia.famousFor: Coffee, emeralds, incredible biodiversity, and colorful cities like Cartagena
landmarks: Cartagena's Old Walled City — a colorful colonial city on the Caribbean coast
Cocora Valley — home to the world's tallest palm trees
Lost City (Ciudad Perdida) — an ancient city older than Machu Picchu

geography.longestRiver: Magdalena River — 949 miles, the most important river in Colombia
geography.tallestPeak: Pico Cristobal Colon — 18,947 feet, Colombia's highest point in the Sierra Nevada

famousFood: Bandeja Paisa — a massive platter with beans, rice, meat, plantain, avocado, and a fried egg
Arepas — grilled corn cakes stuffed with cheese or meat
Empanadas — crispy fried pastries filled with meat and potatoes
festivals: Carnival of Barranquilla — one of the biggest carnivals in the world, second only to Rio de Janeiro
Feria de las Flores (Festival of the Flowers) — a flower festival in Medellin with stunning floral displays
famousPeople: Shakira — global pop superstar whose hips don't lie
Gabriel Garcia Marquez — Nobel Prize-winning author of One Hundred Years of Solitude
James Rodriguez — soccer star who won the Golden Boot at the 2014 World Cup
inventions: The pacemaker — invented by Colombian electrical engineer Jorge Reynolds
Flavor-changing berries research by Colombian food scientists
Transmilenio — one of the most successful bus rapid transit systems in the world
worldRecords: Colombia is the second most biodiverse country in the world — it has more bird species than any other country!
Colombia produces about 70-90% of the world's emeralds
weirdLaws: In Bogota, there used to be a 'car-free day' law where you could get fined for driving on certain days based on your license plate number
uniqueAnimals: Andean Condor — one of the world's largest flying birds with a 10-foot wingspan
Poison Dart Frog — tiny, brilliantly colored frogs found in Colombia's rainforests
Pink River Dolphin — a freshwater dolphin that turns pink as it gets older
sportsAchievements: Colombia won the Copa America in 2001 on home soil
Egan Bernal became the first Colombian to win the Tour de France in 2019
history: Colombia is named after Christopher Columbus, even though he never actually set foot there!
The ancient Muisca people created the legend of 'El Dorado' — the city of gold.
Colombia gained independence from Spain in 1819, led by Simon Bolivar.
Ciudad Perdida (the Lost City) was built around 800 AD — about 650 years before Machu Picchu.
trivia: Colombia has more bird species (over 1,900) than any other country on Earth.
Colombian coffee is considered some of the best in the world — Juan Valdez is a famous fictional coffee farmer.
Colombians get very upset if you spell it 'Columbia' — it's Colombia with an 'o'!

peru.name: Peru
peru.flagDescription: Three vertical stripes: red, white, and red — with a coat of arms in the center of the white stripe
peru.famousFor: Machu Picchu, the Inca Empire, the Amazon Rainforest, and amazing food
landmarks: Machu Picchu — the 'Lost City of the Incas' high in the Andes mountains
Nazca Lines — giant mysterious drawings in the desert visible only from the sky
Lake Titicaca — the highest navigable lake in the world

geography.longestRiver: Ucayali River — 1,014 miles, a major tributary of the Amazon River
geography.tallestPeak: Huascaran — 22,205 feet, Peru's tallest mountain in the Andes

famousFood: Ceviche — raw fish 'cooked' in lime juice with onions and chili peppers, Peru's national dish
Lomo Saltado — stir-fried beef with tomatoes, onions, and french fries over rice
Causa — layered mashed potato dish with avocado, chicken, or tuna
festivals: Inti Raymi — Festival of the Sun, a recreation of the ancient Inca celebration of the winter solstice in Cusco
famousPeople: Pachacuti — the Inca emperor who built Machu Picchu
Mario Vargas Llosa — Nobel Prize-winning author
Paolo Guerrero — Peru's all-time top soccer scorer
inventions: Potato cultivation — Peru domesticated the potato over 7,000 years ago, with over 3,000 varieties
Quipu — an Inca recording system using knotted strings instead of writing
Freeze-drying food — the Incas invented this technique to preserve potatoes at high altitude
worldRecords: Peru's Cotahuasi Canyon is one of the deepest canyons in the world — twice as deep as the Grand Canyon
Lake Titicaca is the highest navigable lake in the world at 12,507 feet above sea level
weirdLaws: In Peru, prisoners are allowed to have guinea pigs as pets — and guinea pigs are also a traditional food!
uniqueAnimals: Llama — the iconic Andean pack animal used by the Incas for thousands of years
Andean Cock-of-the-Rock — Peru's national bird with a brilliant orange crest
Spectacled Bear — South America's only bear species, found in Peru's cloud forests
sportsAchievements: Peru won the Copa America twice (1939 and 1975)
Teofilo Cubillas is considered one of the greatest South American soccer players of all time
history: The Inca Empire was the largest empire in the Americas before the Spanish arrived.
Machu Picchu was built around 1450 AD and was never found by the Spanish conquistadors.
Peru was the center of Spanish colonial power in South America for nearly 300 years.
The potato was first domesticated in Peru over 7,000 years ago — imagine life without fries!
trivia: Peru has over 3,000 varieties of potatoes — more than anywhere else on Earth.
Guinea pig (called 'cuy') is a traditional Peruvian delicacy, not just a pet!
The Nazca Lines are so large that nobody knew they existed until airplanes flew over them in the 1920s.

chile.name: Chile
chile.flagDescription: Two horizontal halves — white on top and red on bottom — with a blue square and white star in the upper left corner
chile.famousFor: Being the longest and narrowest country in the world, the Atacama Desert, and incredible wine
landmarks: Easter Island (Rapa Nui) — mysterious island with hundreds of giant stone heads called Moai
Atacama Desert — the driest place on Earth
Torres del Paine — stunning granite towers in Patagonia

geography.longestRiver: Loa River — 275 miles, the longest river in Chile, flowing through the Atacama Desert
geography.tallestPeak: Ojos del Salado — 22,615 feet, the world's tallest active volcano

famousFood: Empanadas de Pino — baked pastries stuffed with beef, onions, olives, and hard-boiled egg
Completo — a Chilean hot dog loaded with avocado, tomato, and mayonnaise
Curanto — a traditional stew of shellfish, meat, and potatoes cooked in a pit in the ground
festivals: Fiestas Patrias — Chile's Independence Day celebrations in September with rodeos, dancing, and traditional food
famousPeople: Pablo Neruda — Nobel Prize-winning poet and one of the most beloved poets of the 20th century
Gabriela Mistral — the first Latin American to win the Nobel Prize in Literature
Alexis Sanchez — Chilean soccer superstar who played for Barcelona and Arsenal
inventions: The first seismograph in South America was developed in Chile due to frequent earthquakes
Chilean wine production techniques that became world-famous
Contributions to astronomy — Chile hosts the world's most powerful telescopes
worldRecords: The Atacama Desert is the driest place on Earth — some spots have never recorded rain!
Chile experienced the most powerful earthquake ever recorded — a 9.5 magnitude in 1960
weirdLaws: In Chile, you are legally required to vote in elections once you are registered — not voting can result in a fine
uniqueAnimals: Huemul — an endangered South Andean deer that appears on Chile's coat of arms
Pudu — the world's smallest deer, standing only about 15 inches tall
Andean Flamingo — bright pink flamingos living at extreme altitudes in northern Chile
sportsAchievements: Chile won the Copa America in 2015 and 2016, their first-ever major international soccer trophies
Marcelo Rios became the first Latin American tennis player ranked number one in the world
history: Easter Island's mysterious Moai statues were carved between 1250 and 1500 AD — and scientists still debate how they were moved.
Chile gained independence from Spain in 1818, led by Bernardo O'Higgins.
The 1960 Chilean earthquake was the most powerful ever recorded at 9.5 magnitude.
Chile stretches 2,653 miles from north to south but is only 110 miles wide on average!
trivia: Chile is so long that if you placed it across Europe, it would stretch from Norway to Africa.
Parts of the Atacama Desert look so much like Mars that NASA tests Mars rovers there.
Chile has two Nobel Prize-winning poets — more per capita than almost any country!

netherlands.name: Netherlands
netherlands.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes of red, white, and blue from top to bottom
netherlands.famousFor: Tulips, windmills, canals, and being one of the flattest countries in the world.

geography.longestRiver: Rhine — 820 miles total, flows through the Netherlands before emptying into the North Sea
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Scenery — 2,828 feet, located on the Caribbean island of Saba (in the European Netherlands, Vaalserberg at 1,059 feet)

famousFood: Stroopwafel — thin waffle cookies with caramel syrup filling
Bitterballen — crispy deep-fried meatball snacks
Poffertjes — fluffy mini pancakes dusted with powdered sugar
festivals: King's Day — the whole country turns orange on April 27 to celebrate the King's birthday
famousPeople: Rembrandt van Rijn — one of the greatest painters in history
Vincent van Gogh — legendary artist who painted Starry Night
