Anne Frank — young girl whose diary about hiding during WWII is read worldwide
inventions: Microscope
Bluetooth technology
Wi-Fi technology
worldRecords: Has the tallest population on Earth with an average male height of about 6 feet
More bicycles than people — roughly 23 million bikes for 17.9 million people
weirdLaws: It is illegal to not have a bike light on your bicycle at night — police give out fines!
uniqueAnimals: Dutch rabbit — a small, gentle breed originally from the Netherlands
Friesian horse — a beautiful, powerful black horse breed from Friesland
sportsAchievements: Won the most gold medals in speed skating in Olympic history
history: The Dutch East India Company, founded in 1602, was the world's first publicly traded company
During the 1600s Dutch Golden Age, the Netherlands was the wealthiest nation on Earth
About one-third of the country lies below sea level — the Dutch built dikes and pumped water out to create dry land
The Netherlands was occupied by Nazi Germany during WWII from 1940 to 1945
trivia: The country's name means 'low lands' because much of it is below sea level
The Dutch eat over 14 million herring fish per year, often raw!
There are more than 1,000 windmills still standing in the Netherlands

switzerland.name: Switzerland
switzerland.flagDescription: A white cross on a red square background — one of only two square national flags in the world
switzerland.famousFor: Chocolate, cheese, watches, the Alps, and being a neutral country for over 200 years.

geography.longestRiver: Rhine — 233 miles within Switzerland, starts in the Swiss Alps
geography.tallestPeak: Dufourspitze — 15,203 feet, the highest point in Switzerland in the Monte Rosa range

famousFood: Fondue — melted cheese served in a pot, eaten by dipping bread
Rösti — crispy shredded potato pancake
Swiss chocolate — some of the world's finest chocolate, invented milk chocolate in 1875
festivals: Fasnacht (Basel Carnival) — a colorful three-day festival with masks, lanterns, and music
famousPeople: Albert Einstein — developed the theory of relativity while living in Bern
Roger Federer — one of the greatest tennis players of all time
Henry Dunant — founded the Red Cross and won the first Nobel Peace Prize
inventions: Milk chocolate
Swiss Army knife
Velcro
worldRecords: Has the longest tunnel in the world — the Gotthard Base Tunnel at 35.5 miles
Consumes more chocolate per person than any other country — about 20 pounds per year
weirdLaws: In Switzerland, it is illegal to own just one guinea pig — they must be kept in pairs because they get lonely!
uniqueAnimals: Alpine ibex — a wild goat with huge curved horns that lives high in the Swiss Alps
St. Bernard dog — a giant rescue dog breed originally trained to find lost travelers in the Alps
sportsAchievements: Roger Federer won 20 Grand Slam tennis titles, the most by any male player at the time of his retirement
history: Switzerland has not fought in a war since 1815, making it one of the most peaceful countries
The Red Cross was founded in Geneva in 1863 and its symbol is a reversed Swiss flag
Swiss women did not get the right to vote in all elections until 1990
Switzerland is not a member of the European Union but sits right in the middle of Europe
trivia: Switzerland has enough nuclear bunkers to shelter its entire population
The country has four official languages: German, French, Italian, and Romansh
Swiss trains are so punctual that a delay of more than 3 minutes counts as late

sweden.name: Sweden
sweden.flagDescription: A yellow (gold) cross on a blue background, with the cross shifted to the left
sweden.famousFor: Vikings, IKEA furniture, pop music (ABBA), meatballs, and the Nobel Prize.

geography.longestRiver: Klarälven-Göta älv — 447 miles, the longest river system in Sweden
geography.tallestPeak: Kebnekaise — 6,909 feet, the highest mountain in Sweden located in Lapland

famousFood: Swedish meatballs — small seasoned meatballs served with cream sauce and lingonberry jam
Kanelbullar — cinnamon buns so popular they have their own national day on October 4
Smörgåsbord — a buffet of many Swedish dishes including herring, cheeses, and cold meats
festivals: Midsummer — Swedes dance around a flower-covered maypole on the longest day of the year
Nobel Prize ceremony — held every December 10 in Stockholm to honor the world's greatest achievements
famousPeople: Alfred Nobel — invented dynamite and created the Nobel Prizes
Greta Thunberg — teenage climate activist who inspired millions worldwide
Zlatan Ibrahimovic — one of the most famous soccer players in history
inventions: Dynamite
Spotify music streaming
The three-point seatbelt
worldRecords: Home to the world's first ice hotel, rebuilt from ice and snow every winter since 1989
Has the oldest national park in Europe — Sarek, established in 1909
weirdLaws: In Sweden, it is illegal to spontaneously dance in public without a permit — venues need a special dance license!
uniqueAnimals: Swedish Vallhund — a small Viking dog breed that looks like a wolf-corgi mix
Arctic fox — a fluffy white fox that lives in the mountains of northern Sweden
Wolverine — a fierce, bear-like mammal found in Sweden's northern forests
sportsAchievements: Won the FIFA Women's World Cup silver medal multiple times and Olympic gold in women's soccer
history: The Vikings came from Sweden, Norway, and Denmark and explored as far as North America around 1000 AD
Sweden has not been in a war since 1814, one of the longest stretches of peace in history
Alfred Nobel created the Nobel Prize in 1895 after feeling guilty about inventing dynamite
Sweden was one of the first countries to allow freedom of the press in 1766
trivia: In northern Sweden, the sun does not set for weeks during summer — it is called the Midnight Sun
Sweden recycles so well that it imports trash from other countries to burn for energy
IKEA's founder started selling matches at age 5 and began IKEA at age 17

portugal.name: Portugal
portugal.flagDescription: Vertical green stripe on the left and larger red section on the right, with the national coat of arms at the border
portugal.famousFor: Explorers who sailed the world, pastéis de nata custard tarts, and Cristiano Ronaldo.

geography.longestRiver: Tagus — 645 miles total, the longest river on the Iberian Peninsula, flowing through Lisbon
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Pico — 7,713 feet, a volcanic peak on Pico Island in the Azores

famousFood: Pastéis de nata — flaky custard tarts with a crispy caramelized top
Bacalhau — dried salted cod prepared in over 365 different recipes, one for every day of the year
Francesinha — a massive meat sandwich covered in melted cheese and spicy tomato-beer sauce
festivals: Festa de São João — the city of Porto celebrates with plastic hammers, grilled sardines, and fireworks
famousPeople: Cristiano Ronaldo — one of the greatest soccer players ever with over 800 career goals
Vasco da Gama — explorer who found the sea route from Europe to India in 1498
Ferdinand Magellan — led the first expedition to sail around the entire world
inventions: Tempura cooking technique (brought to Japan)
The carrack sailing ship
Pre-paid phone cards
worldRecords: Has the longest bridge in Europe — the Vasco da Gama Bridge at 10.7 miles
The oldest bookstore in the world, Livraria Bertrand in Lisbon, opened in 1732
weirdLaws: It is technically illegal to pee in the ocean in Portugal — though it is nearly impossible to enforce!
uniqueAnimals: Iberian lynx — one of the world's rarest wild cats, found in parts of Portugal
Portuguese Water Dog — a curly-haired dog bred to help fishermen, the same breed as the Obama family's dogs
sportsAchievements: Won the UEFA European Championship (Euro 2016) for the first time in the country's history
history: Portuguese explorers in the 1400s-1500s were the first Europeans to reach Brazil, Africa's coast, India, and Japan
Portugal and England have the oldest active alliance in the world, signed in 1373
A massive earthquake in 1755 destroyed most of Lisbon and caused a tsunami felt across the Atlantic
Portugal was one of the first colonial empires and the last European country to give up its African colonies in 1975
trivia: Portuguese is spoken by over 250 million people worldwide, making it the 6th most spoken language
Portugal legalized all drugs in 2001 and focused on treatment instead of prison
The country produces about half of the world's cork from its cork oak trees

ireland.name: Ireland
ireland.flagDescription: Three vertical stripes of green, white, and orange from left to right
ireland.famousFor: Rolling green hills, Celtic culture, leprechaun legends, and having more sheep than people.

geography.longestRiver: Shannon — 224 miles, the longest river in Ireland and the British Isles
geography.tallestPeak: Carrauntoohil — 3,407 feet, located in County Kerry in the southwest

famousFood: Irish soda bread — a simple bread made with baking soda instead of yeast
Irish stew — a hearty lamb or beef stew with potatoes, carrots, and onions
Boxty — traditional Irish potato pancakes
festivals: St. Patrick's Day — celebrated worldwide on March 17 with green clothing, parades, and shamrocks
famousPeople: Oscar Wilde — brilliant writer and playwright known for his sharp wit
Bono — lead singer of the rock band U2 and humanitarian
Saoirse Ronan — award-winning actress from films like Lady Bird and Little Women
inventions: Color photography
Submarine (designed by John Philip Holland)
Flavored potato chips (crisps)
worldRecords: The longest place name in Ireland is Muckanaghederdauhaulia — try saying that five times fast!
Ireland has won the Eurovision Song Contest 7 times, more than any other country
weirdLaws: Technically, under old Irish law, you must yield the right of way to a funeral procession — even in a car!
uniqueAnimals: Irish hare — a large hare found only in Ireland, it does not turn white in winter like other hares
Connemara pony — a sturdy, athletic pony breed native to western Ireland
sportsAchievements: Hurling, an Irish sport over 3,000 years old, is considered the fastest field sport in the world
history: The Great Famine of 1845-1852 caused Ireland's population to drop by 25% as people starved or emigrated
Ireland gained independence from Britain in 1922 after centuries of British rule
Ancient Celtic monks preserved many important books and learning during Europe's Dark Ages
Newgrange, a tomb older than the Egyptian pyramids, was built around 3200 BC
trivia: There are about 70 million people of Irish descent worldwide — more than 10 times Ireland's population
Ireland has no snakes — legend says St. Patrick drove them all out
Halloween originated from the ancient Irish Celtic festival of Samhain

poland.name: Poland
poland.flagDescription: Two horizontal stripes — white on top and red on the bottom
poland.famousFor: Pierogi dumplings, Copernicus, Marie Curie, and the historic city of Krakow.

geography.longestRiver: Vistula — 651 miles, flows through Warsaw and Krakow to the Baltic Sea
geography.tallestPeak: Rysy — 8,199 feet, located in the Tatra Mountains on the border with Slovakia

famousFood: Pierogi — stuffed dumplings filled with potato, cheese, meat, or fruit
Bigos — a hearty stew of sauerkraut, cabbage, and various meats called Hunter's Stew
Pączki — rich filled donuts traditionally eaten before Lent
festivals: Wianki (Wreaths Festival) — on Midsummer night, girls float flower wreaths on rivers for good luck
famousPeople: Marie Curie — the first person to win two Nobel Prizes, discovered radium and polonium
Nicolaus Copernicus — proved that the Earth revolves around the Sun
Frédéric Chopin — one of the greatest piano composers in history
inventions: Bulletproof vest (by Casimir Zeglen)
Kerosene lamp
The modern oil refinery
worldRecords: The Wieliczka Salt Mine is one of the world's oldest salt mines, operating since the 1200s
Poland has the largest castle in the world by land area — Malbork Castle
weirdLaws: In Poland, Winnie the Pooh was once banned from a playground for being inappropriately dressed — he wears no pants!
uniqueAnimals: European bison (wisent) — the heaviest land animal in Europe, saved from extinction in Poland's Białowieża Forest
Polish Tatra Sheepdog — a large, fluffy white dog bred to guard sheep in the mountains
sportsAchievements: Robert Lewandowski scored 5 goals in 9 minutes in a 2015 Bundesliga match, a record
history: Poland was erased from the map for 123 years (1795-1918) when neighboring empires divided it up
Warsaw was almost completely destroyed during WWII and was rebuilt brick by brick from old paintings and photos
The Solidarity movement in Poland in the 1980s helped end communism in Eastern Europe
Poland's constitution of 1791 was the first modern constitution in Europe
trivia: Marie Curie named the element Polonium after Poland, her homeland
Poland is one of the top producers of amber in the world — sometimes called Baltic Gold
Polish has some of the hardest-to-pronounce words in any European language

iceland.name: Iceland
iceland.flagDescription: Blue background with a red cross outlined in white, shifted to the left
iceland.famousFor: Volcanoes, glaciers, geysers, the Northern Lights, and being the land of fire and ice.

geography.longestRiver: Þjórsá — 143 miles, Iceland's longest river fed by glacial meltwater
geography.tallestPeak: Hvannadalshnjúkur — 6,921 feet, part of the Vatnajökull glacier

famousFood: Skyr — a thick, creamy dairy product similar to yogurt that Vikings ate over 1,000 years ago
Pylsur (Icelandic hot dog) — made with lamb, topped with crispy onions and special sauces
Kleinur — twisted Icelandic donuts flavored with cardamom
festivals: Þorrablót — a midwinter festival where Icelanders eat unusual traditional foods like fermented shark
famousPeople: Leif Erikson — Viking explorer who reached North America 500 years before Columbus
Björk — world-famous singer and artist known for creative and unique music
Halldór Laxness — Icelandic author who won the Nobel Prize in Literature
inventions: The Icelandic sagas (some of the first novels ever written)
Geothermal energy heating systems
The Icelandic naming committee that approves baby names
worldRecords: The world's oldest parliament, the Althing, was founded in Iceland in 930 AD
Iceland has more books published per person than any other country in the world
weirdLaws: Iceland has an official Naming Committee — parents must choose baby names from an approved list or get special permission!
uniqueAnimals: Icelandic horse — a small, sturdy breed that has been isolated on the island for over 1,000 years
Arctic fox — the only native land mammal in Iceland, it arrived before the Vikings
Atlantic puffin — millions of these colorful seabirds nest on Iceland's cliffs each summer
sportsAchievements: Iceland became the smallest country ever to qualify for the FIFA World Cup in 2018 with only 335,000 people
history: Vikings from Norway settled Iceland around 870 AD
The Althing, founded in 930 AD, is the world's oldest surviving parliament
Iceland was under Danish rule for centuries and gained full independence in 1944
In 2010, the Eyjafjallajökull volcano erupted and shut down air travel across Europe for weeks
trivia: Iceland heats 90% of its homes using geothermal energy from underground hot water
There are no mosquitoes in Iceland — one of the few places on Earth without them
Icelanders still use a phone book sorted by first names because everyone uses patronymic last names

morocco.name: Morocco
morocco.flagDescription: Red background with a green five-pointed star (pentacle) in the center
morocco.famousFor: Colorful markets (souks), the Sahara Desert, mint tea, and beautiful mosaic tile art.

geography.longestRiver: Draa — 683 miles, flows from the Atlas Mountains toward the Sahara Desert
geography.tallestPeak: Toubkal — 13,671 feet, the highest peak in North Africa, in the Atlas Mountains

famousFood: Tagine — a slow-cooked stew of meat, vegetables, and spices cooked in a cone-shaped clay pot
Couscous — tiny steamed grain served with vegetables and meat, traditionally eaten on Fridays
Pastilla — a flaky pastry pie filled with spiced pigeon or chicken, almonds, and powdered sugar
festivals: Gnaoua World Music Festival — a famous music festival in Essaouira blending African and world music
famousPeople: Ibn Battuta — one of the greatest travelers in history, journeyed over 75,000 miles in the 1300s
Fatima al-Fihri — founded the world's oldest university, the University of al-Qarawiyyin, in 859 AD
Gad Elmaleh — internationally known comedian and actor
inventions: The world's oldest university (University of al-Qarawiyyin, 859 AD)
Argan oil beauty products
Zellige mosaic tilework
worldRecords: Home to the University of al-Qarawiyyin, recognized by UNESCO as the oldest existing university in the world
The Noor-Ouarzazate solar complex is one of the world's largest concentrated solar power plants
weirdLaws: In Morocco, you can get in trouble for eating in public during Ramadan — it is considered very disrespectful!
uniqueAnimals: Barbary macaque — the only wild monkey in Africa that lives north of the Sahara
Fennec fox — a tiny desert fox with enormous ears, found in Morocco's Sahara region
sportsAchievements: Morocco became the first African and Arab nation to reach the FIFA World Cup semi-finals in 2022
history: Morocco is one of the oldest kingdoms in the world, with a monarchy dating back to 789 AD
The ancient city of Fez was one of the great centers of learning in the medieval world
Morocco was the first country to recognize the United States as an independent nation in 1777
The country was divided between France and Spain as protectorates and regained independence in 1956
trivia: Moroccan mint tea is poured from high above the glass to create a frothy top — it is an art form
The blue city of Chefchaouen has almost every building painted in shades of blue
Morocco is only about 8 miles from Spain, separated by the Strait of Gibraltar

ethiopia.name: Ethiopia
ethiopia.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes of green, yellow, and red with a blue circle and golden star in the center
ethiopia.famousFor: Being the birthplace of coffee, one of the oldest civilizations in the world, and legendary long-distance runners.

geography.longestRiver: Blue Nile (Abay) — 900 miles, starts at Lake Tana and provides most of the Nile's water
geography.tallestPeak: Ras Dashen — 14,928 feet, one of the highest peaks in Africa, in the Simien Mountains

famousFood: Injera — a large, spongy flatbread used as both plate and utensil to scoop up stews
Doro Wat — a spicy chicken stew with hard-boiled eggs, the national dish
Ethiopian coffee — served in an elaborate ceremony with roasting, grinding, and three rounds of pouring
festivals: Timkat — the Ethiopian celebration of Epiphany with colorful processions, music, and holy water
famousPeople: Haile Selassie — the last emperor of Ethiopia, revered as a symbol of African independence
Abebe Bikila — won the Olympic marathon barefoot in 1960, the first Black African to win Olympic gold
Lucy (Dinkinesh) — a 3.2-million-year-old human ancestor skeleton found in Ethiopia in 1974
inventions: Coffee (discovered by an Ethiopian goat herder)
The Ge'ez script, one of Africa's oldest writing systems
Injera flatbread cooking technique
worldRecords: Ethiopia has its own calendar that is 7-8 years behind the rest of the world — when it was 2000 elsewhere, it was 1993 in Ethiopia!
Has one of the hottest inhabited places on Earth — the Danakil Depression reaches over 120°F
weirdLaws: Ethiopia's New Year is on September 11 (or 12 in leap years), and the country is currently in a different year than the rest of the world!
uniqueAnimals: Gelada monkey — sometimes called the bleeding-heart monkey for its red chest patch, found only in Ethiopia
Ethiopian wolf — the rarest canid in the world, found only in the Ethiopian highlands
Walia ibex — an endangered wild goat found only in Ethiopia's Simien Mountains
sportsAchievements: Ethiopian runners have won more Olympic gold medals in long-distance running than almost any other country
history: Ethiopia is one of the oldest nations in the world, with a civilization going back over 3,000 years
It was never colonized by a European power, one of only two African countries to maintain independence
The Kingdom of Aksum was one of the great empires of the ancient world
Ethiopia's Lalibela churches were carved entirely from solid rock in the 12th century
trivia: Legend says a goat herder named Kaldi discovered coffee when his goats started dancing after eating coffee berries
Ethiopia runs on a 13-month calendar — the 13th month has only 5 or 6 days
Addis Ababa means 'new flower' in Amharic and sits at 7,726 feet, making it the third-highest capital in the world

cuba.name: Cuba
cuba.flagDescription: Five alternating blue and white stripes with a red triangle on the left containing a white star
cuba.famousFor: Classic old cars from the 1950s, salsa music, cigars, and colorful colonial architecture.

geography.longestRiver: Cauto — 230 miles, the longest river in Cuba flowing through eastern Cuba
geography.tallestPeak: Pico Turquino — 6,476 feet, located in the Sierra Maestra mountains

famousFood: Ropa vieja — shredded beef in tomato sauce, the name means 'old clothes' because of how the meat looks
Cuban sandwich — pressed sandwich with roasted pork, ham, Swiss cheese, pickles, and mustard
Tostones — twice-fried green plantain slices, crispy and delicious
festivals: Havana Carnival — a huge summer street party with music, dancing, and elaborate floats and costumes
famousPeople: José Martí — Cuba's national hero, a poet and leader of independence from Spain
Celia Cruz — the Queen of Salsa, one of the most famous Latin music performers ever
José Raúl Capablanca — one of the greatest chess champions in history
inventions: The daiquiri cocktail (frozen fruit drink version)
Cigar rolling techniques perfected over centuries
The mambo dance style
worldRecords: Cuba has the highest doctor-to-patient ratio in the world
The world's smallest bird, the bee hummingbird, is found only in Cuba — it is about 2 inches long
weirdLaws: For decades, Cubans could not legally buy or sell houses — they could only trade them with each other!
uniqueAnimals: Bee hummingbird — the world's smallest bird at just 2 inches, found only in Cuba
Cuban hutia — a large rodent that looks like a giant guinea pig, native to Cuba
Cuban crocodile — a rare, aggressive crocodile found only in Cuba's Zapata Swamp
sportsAchievements: Cuba has won more Olympic gold medals in boxing than any other country
history: Cuba was a Spanish colony for nearly 400 years before gaining independence in 1902
Fidel Castro led a revolution in 1959 that turned Cuba into a communist country
The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 brought the US and Soviet Union to the brink of nuclear war
Thousands of classic 1950s American cars still drive through Cuba's streets because trade restrictions prevented new car imports
trivia: Cuba is the largest island in the Caribbean Sea
Baseball, not soccer, is Cuba's most popular sport — Cuban players are among the best in the world
Cuba has one of the highest literacy rates in the world at over 99%

jamaica.name: Jamaica
jamaica.flagDescription: A gold diagonal cross dividing the flag into green triangles (top and bottom) and black triangles (left and right)
jamaica.famousFor: Reggae music, Bob Marley, Usain Bolt, jerk chicken, and stunning beaches.

geography.longestRiver: Rio Minho — 58 miles, the longest river in Jamaica
geography.tallestPeak: Blue Mountain Peak — 7,402 feet, famous for the premium coffee grown on its slopes

famousFood: Jerk chicken — chicken marinated in fiery scotch bonnet peppers and allspice, grilled over pimento wood
Ackee and saltfish — the national dish made from a tropical fruit and dried codfish
Jamaican patty — a flaky golden pastry filled with spiced meat
festivals: Reggae Sumfest — one of the world's biggest reggae music festivals held in Montego Bay
famousPeople: Usain Bolt — the fastest human ever, set the 100m world record at 9.58 seconds
Bob Marley — the king of reggae music whose songs like 'One Love' are known worldwide
Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce — one of the fastest women in Olympic sprinting history
inventions: Reggae music
Jerk cooking technique
Jamaican Blue Mountain Coffee processing
worldRecords: Usain Bolt's 100m record of 9.58 seconds and 200m record of 19.19 seconds are both held by a Jamaican
Jamaica is the smallest country to have won a Winter Olympics bobsled qualification — their 1988 bobsled team inspired the movie Cool Runnings
weirdLaws: In Jamaica, it is technically illegal to swear in public — you can be fined for using bad language on the street!
uniqueAnimals: Doctor Bird (red-billed streamertail) — a dazzling hummingbird with long tail feathers found only in Jamaica
Jamaican iguana — one of the rarest lizards on Earth, once thought to be extinct
sportsAchievements: Jamaica, a tiny island nation, has won more Olympic sprint medals than countries hundreds of times its size
history: The original inhabitants were the Taíno people before Spanish colonization in 1509
Jamaica was a British colony for over 300 years before gaining independence in 1962
Port Royal was once called the wickedest city on Earth — a pirate capital that sank into the sea during a 1692 earthquake
Marcus Garvey, born in Jamaica, was a key leader of the Black empowerment movement worldwide
trivia: Jamaica was the first Caribbean country to compete in the Winter Olympics — with a bobsled team in 1988
The island has more churches per square mile than almost any other country in the world
Blue Mountain Coffee from Jamaica is among the most expensive coffees in the world

uae.name: United Arab Emirates
uae.flagDescription: A vertical red stripe on the left with three horizontal stripes of green, white, and black
uae.famousFor: The tallest building in the world (Burj Khalifa), luxury shopping, futuristic cities, and desert adventures.

geography.longestRiver: The UAE has no permanent rivers — it is mostly desert with seasonal wadis (dry riverbeds that fill during rain)
geography.tallestPeak: Jebel Jais — 6,345 feet, the highest point in the UAE in the Hajar Mountains

famousFood: Shawarma — seasoned meat shaved from a rotating spit, wrapped in flatbread with garlic sauce
Al Machboos — a spiced rice dish with meat, the UAE's national dish
Luqaimat — sweet fried dough balls drizzled with date syrup, a popular dessert
festivals: Dubai Shopping Festival — a month-long event with massive sales, fireworks, and entertainment
famousPeople: Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan — founding father of the UAE who united seven emirates in 1971
Hazza Al Mansouri — the first Emirati astronaut who went to the International Space Station in 2019
Sultan Al Neyadi — the first Arab astronaut to complete a long-duration space mission
inventions: Palm Jumeirah, the world's largest artificial island
Indoor ski slopes in the desert
The world's first 3D-printed office building
worldRecords: The Burj Khalifa is the tallest building in the world at 2,717 feet with 163 floors
Dubai Frame is the largest picture frame structure in the world at 492 feet tall
weirdLaws: In the UAE, your car must be kept clean — you can get fined for driving a dirty car!
uniqueAnimals: Arabian oryx — a beautiful white antelope that was once extinct in the wild and was brought back through breeding programs
Sand gazelle — a graceful desert antelope adapted to survive extreme heat with very little water
sportsAchievements: The UAE hosts the Abu Dhabi Grand Prix, one of the most glamorous Formula 1 races in the world
history: The UAE was formed in 1971 when seven emirates united into one country — it is one of the youngest nations
Before oil was discovered in the 1950s, the economy depended on pearl diving and fishing
Dubai went from a small fishing village to one of the most modern cities on Earth in about 50 years
Ancient civilizations have lived in the area for over 5,000 years, trading with Mesopotamia
trivia: Dubai's police force drives supercars including Lamborghinis, Ferraris, and Bugattis
The UAE has the world's longest zipline at Jebel Jais, stretching 1.76 miles
About 90% of the UAE's population are expats — people from other countries living and working there

venezuela.name: Venezuela
venezuela.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes of yellow, blue, and red with eight white stars in an arc on the blue stripe
venezuela.famousFor: Angel Falls (the world's tallest waterfall), oil reserves, beauty pageant winners, and arepa bread.

geography.longestRiver: Orinoco — 1,330 miles, one of the longest rivers in South America
geography.tallestPeak: Pico Bolívar — 16,332 feet, located in the Andes in Mérida state

famousFood: Arepa — a cornmeal patty split open and stuffed with cheese, beans, or meat, eaten daily
Pabellón criollo — the national dish of shredded beef, black beans, rice, and fried plantains
Tequeños — fried cheese sticks wrapped in dough, a beloved party snack
festivals: Carnival of El Callao — a vibrant, UNESCO-recognized carnival with calypso music and colorful costumes
famousPeople: Simón Bolívar — 'The Liberator' who freed six South American countries from Spanish rule
Miguel Cabrera — one of the greatest baseball hitters in Major League history
Carolina Herrera — world-famous fashion designer from Caracas
inventions: The first colored TV transmission system (by Guillermo González Camarena, partly developed in Venezuela)
Biodiesel from waste oil processes
Arepa cuisine that spread across Latin America
worldRecords: Angel Falls is the world's tallest waterfall at 3,212 feet — water falls so far that much of it turns to mist before reaching the bottom
Venezuela has won more international beauty pageant titles (Miss Universe, Miss World) than almost any other country
weirdLaws: In Venezuela, it is against the law to go out without carrying your national ID card — you must have it at all times!
uniqueAnimals: Venezuelan poodle moth — a strange, fluffy white moth discovered in 2009 that looks like a stuffed animal
Orinoco crocodile — one of the rarest reptiles in the world, found in Venezuela's Orinoco River
Canaima poison dart frog — a tiny, brightly colored frog found in Venezuela's rainforests
sportsAchievements: Venezuela has produced more Major League Baseball players than any country outside the US and the Dominican Republic
history: Simón Bolívar, born in Caracas, led the independence of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia
Venezuela was the first country in South America to declare independence from Spain in 1811
The country sits on the largest proven oil reserves in the world
Christopher Columbus landed in Venezuela in 1498, naming it 'Little Venice' because stilt houses reminded him of Venice, Italy
trivia: Lightning strikes over Lake Maracaibo about 300 nights per year — a phenomenon called Catatumbo Lightning
Venezuela's Angel Falls is so remote that it was not known to the outside world until a pilot flew over it in 1933
Baseball, not soccer, is the most popular sport in Venezuela — one of the few South American countries where this is true

ukraine.name: Ukraine
ukraine.flagDescription: Two horizontal stripes: blue (top) representing the sky, and yellow (bottom) representing wheat fields
ukraine.famousFor: Being the breadbasket of Europe with vast golden wheat fields and sunflower farms
landmarks: Kyiv Pechersk Lavra (Monastery of the Caves)

geography.longestRiver: Dnipro River — 1,423 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Hoverla — 6,762 feet

famousFood: Borscht — a vibrant beet soup with a bright red-purple color, served with sour cream
Varenyky — dumplings stuffed with potatoes, cheese, or cherries
Chicken Kyiv — crispy breaded chicken stuffed with garlic butter
festivals: Malanka — a colorful New Year's celebration where people dress in wild costumes and parade through the streets
famousPeople: Taras Shevchenko — beloved national poet and painter who helped shape the Ukrainian language
Sergei Korolev — rocket scientist who launched the first human into space
Andriy Shevchenko — legendary soccer star who won the Ballon d'Or
inventions: The first helicopter design (Igor Sikorsky)
Astronaut training (Sergei Korolev's rocket program launched Yuri Gagarin)
The gas lamp (Ignacy Lukasiewicz, born in what is now Ukraine)
Wheat hybridization techniques that helped feed the world
worldRecords: Ukraine is the largest country located entirely within Europe
The Arsenalna metro station in Kyiv is the deepest metro station in the world at 346 feet underground
weirdLaws: It is technically illegal to use foul language in public places in Ukraine
uniqueAnimals: European Bison (wisent) — once nearly extinct, now protected in Ukrainian forests
Steppe Eagle — a powerful bird of prey found in Ukraine's grasslands
Marbled Polecat — a colorful and rare weasel-like animal
sportsAchievements: Vitali and Wladimir Klitschko — brothers who both became world heavyweight boxing champions
Ukraine won 11 gold medals at the 2024 Paralympics
history: Kyiv was one of the most important cities in medieval Europe and the center of a powerful kingdom called Kyivan Rus'.
Ukraine was once called the 'breadbasket of Europe' because it grew so much wheat it helped feed the entire continent.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster in 1986 was the worst nuclear accident in history.
Ukraine declared independence from the Soviet Union in 1991.
trivia: Ukraine grows more sunflowers than almost any other country in the world!
The deepest metro station on Earth is in Kyiv — it takes 5 minutes to ride the escalator down.
The word 'Ukraine' comes from an old Slavic word meaning 'borderland.'

iraq.name: Iraq
iraq.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes: red (top), white (middle), green (bottom), with 'Allahu Akbar' (God is Great) written in green Arabic script on the white stripe
iraq.famousFor: Being the Cradle of Civilization — the place where humans first invented writing, built cities, and created laws
landmarks: The Ziggurat of Ur — a massive stepped temple over 4,000 years old
The Marshlands of southern Iraq — a unique wetland ecosystem

geography.longestRiver: Euphrates River — 1,740 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Cheekha Dar — 11,847 feet

famousFood: Masgouf — grilled carp fish seasoned with tamarind, a dish that's been cooked the same way for thousands of years
Kleicha — date-filled cookies that are Iraq's national cookie
Dolma — grape leaves stuffed with rice, meat, and spices
famousPeople: Hammurabi — ancient king who created one of the world's first written sets of laws
Zaha Hadid — world-famous architect who designed incredible futuristic buildings
Al-Khwarizmi — mathematician who invented algebra (the word 'algorithm' comes from his name!)
inventions: Writing — the Sumerians of ancient Iraq invented the first writing system called cuneiform over 5,000 years ago
The wheel — first used in Mesopotamia around 3500 BCE
Algebra — developed by the mathematician Al-Khwarizmi in Baghdad
The 60-minute hour and 60-second minute — invented by ancient Babylonians
The first schools and libraries in human history
worldRecords: Iraq (ancient Mesopotamia) is home to the oldest known writing in the world — over 5,000 years old
The Code of Hammurabi is one of the oldest written sets of laws ever discovered
weirdLaws: In ancient Babylon, if a house collapsed and killed the owner, the builder could be put to death under Hammurabi's Code
uniqueAnimals: Iraqi Smooth-coated Otter — found in the marshlands of southern Iraq
Persian Fallow Deer — an extremely rare deer once thought to be extinct
Basra Reed Warbler — a bird that lives only in Iraq's marshes
sportsAchievements: Iraq won the Asian Cup in soccer in 2007, uniting the whole country in celebration
Iraqi weightlifters have competed in multiple Olympic Games
history: Ancient Mesopotamia (modern Iraq) is called the Cradle of Civilization because it's where humans first built cities over 6,000 years ago.
The Sumerians in Iraq invented writing around 3200 BCE using wedge-shaped marks on clay tablets called cuneiform.
Baghdad was once the largest and most advanced city in the world, with a famous library called the House of Wisdom.
The Hanging Gardens of Babylon — one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World — were said to be located in Iraq.
trivia: The reason we have 60 minutes in an hour and 360 degrees in a circle is because ancient Babylonians in Iraq used a base-60 number system!
Iraq sits between two famous rivers — the Tigris and Euphrates — and this region was called Mesopotamia, meaning 'land between the rivers.'
The world's first known author was a woman named Enheduanna, a poet and priestess in ancient Iraq around 2300 BCE.

bangladesh.name: Bangladesh
bangladesh.flagDescription: A red circle slightly off-center on a dark green background, representing the sun rising over the lush green land
bangladesh.famousFor: The Sundarbans — the largest mangrove forest in the world and home to the Royal Bengal Tiger
landmarks: Lalbagh Fort — a 17th-century Mughal fortress in Dhaka
Cox's Bazar — the longest natural sea beach in the world

geography.longestRiver: Padma River (part of the Ganges) — 75 miles within Bangladesh
geography.tallestPeak: Keokradong — 4,035 feet

famousFood: Hilsa fish curry — the national fish cooked in mustard sauce, a beloved dish across the country
Biryani — fragrant spiced rice with meat, a favorite for celebrations
Pitha — traditional rice cakes filled with coconut and date palm syrup
festivals: Pohela Boishakh — the Bengali New Year celebrated with colorful parades, music, and feasting
famousPeople: Rabindranath Tagore — Nobel Prize-winning poet who wrote Bangladesh's national anthem
Muhammad Yunus — Nobel Peace Prize winner who invented microloans to help poor people start businesses
Shakib Al Hasan — world-famous cricket all-rounder
inventions: Microcredit (tiny loans to help poor people) — invented by Muhammad Yunus
Muslin fabric — ancient Bangladesh made the finest cotton cloth in the world, so thin it was called 'woven air'
Jute processing — Bangladesh is called the 'Land of Jute' and pioneered jute fiber production
worldRecords: Cox's Bazar is the longest unbroken sandy sea beach in the world at 75 miles long
The Sundarbans is the largest mangrove forest in the world
weirdLaws: Children aged 15 and older can be jailed for cheating on their final exams in Bangladesh
uniqueAnimals: Royal Bengal Tiger — the majestic national animal found in the Sundarbans
Ganges River Dolphin — a rare freshwater dolphin that is nearly blind
Hoolock Gibbon — the only ape species found in Bangladesh
sportsAchievements: Bangladesh's cricket team earned Test status in 2000 and has grown into a competitive international team
Kabaddi is wildly popular — Bangladesh has won gold medals in the South Asian Games
history: Bangladesh became an independent country in 1971 after a war of liberation from Pakistan.
The Bengali Language Movement of 1952 inspired UNESCO to declare February 21 as International Mother Language Day worldwide.
Ancient Bengal was known for producing the finest muslin fabric in the world — Roman emperors wore it!
Bangladesh is one of the most densely populated countries in the world, with 170 million people in an area smaller than Iowa.
trivia: Bangladesh gave the world International Mother Language Day — February 21 is celebrated globally because Bangladeshis fought to protect their language.
The country is called the 'Land of Rivers' — it has over 700 rivers flowing through it!
Ancient muslin cloth from Bengal was so thin that a 50-foot piece could be folded to fit inside a matchbox.

denmark.name: Denmark
denmark.flagDescription: A white Scandinavian cross on a red background — the oldest national flag still in use today, dating back to 1219
denmark.famousFor: LEGO bricks, Vikings, fairy tales by Hans Christian Andersen, and being one of the happiest countries on Earth
landmarks: The Little Mermaid statue in Copenhagen
Tivoli Gardens — one of the oldest amusement parks in the world
LEGOLAND Billund — the original LEGO theme park

geography.longestRiver: Gudenaa — 98 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mollehoj — 561 feet (Denmark is one of the flattest countries!)

famousFood: Smorrebrod — open-faced sandwiches piled high with toppings like fish, meat, and pickles
Danish pastry — flaky, buttery pastries filled with custard or fruit
Frikadeller — traditional Danish meatballs that families eat for dinner
festivals: Roskilde Festival — one of the largest music festivals in Europe
famousPeople: Hans Christian Andersen — wrote fairy tales like The Little Mermaid, The Ugly Duckling, and The Emperor's New Clothes
Niels Bohr — Nobel Prize-winning physicist who helped us understand atoms
Caroline Wozniacki — tennis champion who was ranked #1 in the world
inventions: LEGO bricks — invented in 1932 in Billund, Denmark (LEGO means 'play well' in Danish)
Google Maps (co-created by Danish brothers Lars and Jens Rasmussen)
The loudspeaker
Insulin treatment for diabetes (developed by Danish scientists)
worldRecords: The Danish flag (Dannebrog) is the oldest national flag still in use — it's been used since 1219!
Denmark was ranked the happiest country in the world multiple times
weirdLaws: In Denmark, you must check under your car for sleeping children before starting the engine (an old law that is technically still on the books)
uniqueAnimals: Mute Swan — the graceful national bird that inspired The Ugly Duckling
Red Deer — the largest wild animal in Denmark
Harbor Porpoise — a small whale found in Danish waters
sportsAchievements: Denmark shocked the world by winning the 1992 European Football Championship — they weren't even supposed to be in the tournament!
Danish sailors have won multiple Olympic gold medals in sailing events
history: The Vikings came from Denmark, Norway, and Sweden — they were fierce warriors and explorers who sailed as far as North America around 1000 CE.
Denmark once controlled a huge empire including Norway, Iceland, Greenland, and parts of England.
During World War II, the Danish people secretly rescued almost all of Denmark's Jewish population by smuggling them to safety in Sweden.
Greenland, the world's largest island, is part of the Kingdom of Denmark.
trivia: LEGO produces about 36 billion bricks per year — enough for every person on Earth to get 5 bricks each!
No place in Denmark is more than 30 miles from the sea.
The Danish concept of 'hygge' (pronounced HOO-gah) means a feeling of cozy togetherness — and there's no exact English word for it!

finland.name: Finland
finland.flagDescription: A blue Nordic cross on a white background, representing the blue lakes and white snow of Finland
finland.famousFor: Santa Claus Village in Rovaniemi, having the best education system in the world, saunas, and the Northern Lights
landmarks: Santa Claus Village in Rovaniemi — the official hometown of Santa Claus
Suomenlinna Sea Fortress — a UNESCO World Heritage Site built on islands near Helsinki
Finnish Lakeland — a region with over 55,000 lakes

geography.longestRiver: Kemijoki River — 342 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Halti — 4,344 feet

famousFood: Karelian pie — rye crust filled with rice porridge, topped with egg butter
Salmon soup (Lohikeitto) — a creamy salmon and potato soup
Salmiakki — salty black licorice that Finns love but most visitors find surprising!
famousPeople: Jean Sibelius — famous composer whose music captures the spirit of Finnish nature
Linus Torvalds — created Linux, the operating system that runs most of the internet's servers and Android phones
Tove Jansson — author and artist who created the beloved Moomins characters
inventions: Linux operating system — created by Linus Torvalds (it runs most web servers and all Android phones!)
The sauna (Finns have been using saunas for over 2,000 years)
Nokia mobile phones — Finland's Nokia was once the world's biggest phone company
Angry Birds — the famous mobile game was created by Finnish company Rovio
worldRecords: Finland has more saunas than cars — about 3.3 million saunas for 5.6 million people!
Finland was ranked the happiest country in the world for 7 years in a row (2018-2024)
weirdLaws: In Finland, speeding fines are based on your income — a millionaire once got a $100,000+ speeding ticket!
uniqueAnimals: Saimaa Ringed Seal — one of the rarest seals in the world, found only in Finland's Lake Saimaa
Wolverine — a fierce and powerful animal that lives in Finland's northern forests
Arctic Fox — a fluffy white fox that survives Finland's freezing winters
sportsAchievements: Finland has won more Olympic medals per capita than almost any country, especially in cross-country skiing and athletics
Finland hosts the Wife Carrying World Championship — yes, it's a real sport!
history: Finland was part of Sweden for about 600 years and then part of Russia for about 100 years before becoming independent in 1917.
Finland's education system is considered the best in the world — kids don't start school until age 7, get very little homework, and have no standardized tests!
Santa Claus officially lives in Rovaniemi, Finland — you can visit him and send letters with an Arctic Circle postmark.
During World War II, tiny Finland held off the massive Soviet Union in the Winter War, shocking the world.
trivia: Finland has about 3.3 million saunas — that's almost one sauna for every two people! Even the Finnish parliament has a sauna.
Finnish kids consistently score among the highest in the world on education tests, even though they have less homework and more recess than most countries.
In Finland, you can see the Northern Lights (Aurora Borealis) up to 200 nights per year in the far north!

tanzania.name: Tanzania
tanzania.flagDescription: A diagonal black stripe bordered by yellow, with green on the upper left and blue on the lower right
tanzania.famousFor: Mount Kilimanjaro, the Serengeti, and the island of Zanzibar

geography.longestRiver: Rufiji River — 373 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Kilimanjaro — 19,341 feet (the tallest mountain in all of Africa!)

famousFood: Ugali — a thick cornmeal porridge eaten with stews and vegetables
Zanzibar Pizza — a street-food crepe stuffed with meat, eggs, and veggies
Pilau — spiced rice cooked with meat and aromatic spices from Zanzibar
festivals: Zanzibar International Film Festival — a celebration of film, music, and art on the island of Zanzibar
famousPeople: Freddie Mercury — legendary Queen singer, born in Zanzibar
Julius Nyerere — founding father and first president of Tanzania
Hasheem Thabeet — first Tanzanian NBA player
inventions: Tingatinga painting — a unique art style using bright colors on flat boards
Tanzanite gemstone — a rare blue-violet gem found only near Kilimanjaro
Swahili language — Tanzania helped standardize Swahili, now spoken by over 100 million people
worldRecords: Mount Kilimanjaro is the tallest free-standing mountain in the world
The Serengeti hosts the largest animal migration on Earth — over 1.5 million wildebeest!
weirdLaws: It is illegal to walk around without being able to prove you have a job or source of income
uniqueAnimals: Wildebeest — over a million migrate through the Serengeti every year
Red Colobus Monkey — an endangered monkey found only on Zanzibar
African Wild Dog — one of the continent's most endangered predators
sportsAchievements: Tanzania has produced world-class long-distance runners in marathons and track events
The country's national soccer team is nicknamed the Kilimanjaro Stars
history: Olduvai Gorge in Tanzania is called the 'Cradle of Mankind' — some of the oldest human fossils ever found come from here!
Zanzibar was once the world's biggest producer of cloves, earning it the nickname 'Spice Island.'
Tanzania was formed in 1964 when Tanganyika and Zanzibar united into one country.
The ancient city of Kilwa was one of the richest trading ports in Africa during the 1200s.
trivia: Freddie Mercury, the lead singer of Queen, was born in Zanzibar, Tanzania!
Tanzanite, a rare gemstone, is found in only one place on Earth — near Mount Kilimanjaro.
The Serengeti's Great Migration is sometimes called the 'Greatest Show on Earth.'

ghana.name: Ghana
ghana.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes: red (top), gold (middle), green (bottom), with a black star in the center
ghana.famousFor: Being the first African country south of the Sahara to gain independence, and its gold and cocoa

geography.longestRiver: Volta River — 994 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Afadjato — 2,904 feet

famousFood: Jollof Rice — a beloved one-pot rice dish with tomatoes and spices (Ghana and Nigeria both claim they make the best!)
Fufu — pounded cassava and plantain dipped in tasty soups
Kelewele — spicy fried plantain cubes, a popular street snack
festivals: Homowo Festival — a harvest festival where people literally 'hoot at hunger' to celebrate overcoming famine
Panafest — a celebration of African culture and heritage held at Cape Coast Castle
famousPeople: Kwame Nkrumah — led Ghana to independence and inspired freedom movements across Africa
Kofi Annan — former Secretary-General of the United Nations and Nobel Peace Prize winner
Michael Essien — world-famous soccer midfielder who played for Chelsea
inventions: Kente cloth — a beautifully woven fabric with patterns that tell stories and represent values
Cocoa farming techniques that helped make chocolate possible worldwide
Adinkra symbols — visual symbols that represent concepts and proverbs
worldRecords: Ghana is one of the world's top producers of cocoa — the key ingredient in chocolate!
Lake Volta is one of the largest artificial lakes in the world by surface area
weirdLaws: It is illegal to step on money in Ghana, as it is considered disrespectful to the national symbols on the currency
uniqueAnimals: Mona Monkey — a playful monkey found in Ghana's rainforests
Royal Python — considered sacred in some Ghanaian communities
Diana Monkey — an endangered monkey named after the Roman goddess
sportsAchievements: Ghana's national team, the Black Stars, has won the Africa Cup of Nations 4 times
Ghana became the first African country to reach the World Cup quarterfinals in 2010
history: In 1957, Ghana became the first country in sub-Saharan Africa to gain independence from colonial rule.
The region was once called the 'Gold Coast' because of its rich gold deposits.
The Ashanti Empire was one of the most powerful kingdoms in West African history.
Cape Coast Castle was a major site in the transatlantic slave trade and is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
trivia: Ghana and Nigeria have a fierce but friendly rivalry over who makes the best Jollof Rice!
Much of the world's chocolate comes from Ghanaian cocoa beans.
The black star on Ghana's flag symbolizes African freedom — it inspired many other African nations.

congo.name: Democratic Republic of the Congo
congo.flagDescription: A sky-blue background with a diagonal red stripe bordered by thin yellow stripes, and a yellow star in the upper left corner
congo.famousFor: The mighty Congo River, vast rainforests, and incredible biodiversity

geography.longestRiver: Congo River — 2,920 miles (the deepest river in the world!)
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Stanley — 16,762 feet

famousFood: Moambe Chicken — chicken cooked in a rich palm nut sauce, the national dish
Fufu — dough made from cassava, served with soups and stews
Liboke — fish or meat wrapped in banana leaves and cooked over a fire
famousPeople: Patrice Lumumba — the first prime minister of the independent Congo
Dikembe Mutombo — legendary NBA shot-blocker known for wagging his finger
Fally Ipupa — internationally famous musician and singer
inventions: Congolese rumba music — a style that influenced music across all of Africa and the world
Ancient ironworking — people in the Congo basin were among the earliest iron smelters in Africa
Coltan mining — the DRC supplies much of the coltan used in smartphones and electronics worldwide
worldRecords: The Congo River is the deepest river in the world, reaching over 720 feet deep!
The DRC has the second-largest rainforest in the world after the Amazon
weirdLaws: In some parts of the DRC, it is considered bad luck to count the number of people in a group out loud
uniqueAnimals: Okapi — a forest giraffe with a striped rear end, found only in the DRC
Bonobo — one of humanity's closest relatives, found nowhere else on Earth
Congo Peafowl — the only true peacock species native to Africa
sportsAchievements: Dikembe Mutombo was inducted into the NBA Hall of Fame and was a 4-time NBA Defensive Player of the Year
The DRC's soccer team (the Leopards) won the Africa Cup of Nations in 1968 and 1974
history: The Congo River basin is home to the second-largest rainforest on Earth.
The Kingdom of Kongo was a powerful central African kingdom from the 1300s to the 1800s.
The DRC gained independence from Belgium in 1960.
Virunga National Park, Africa's oldest national park, protects endangered mountain gorillas.
trivia: The okapi was so mysterious that Western scientists didn't even know it existed until 1901!
Your smartphone probably contains coltan, a mineral largely mined in the DRC.
Kinshasa and Brazzaville (capital of neighboring Republic of Congo) are the two closest capital cities in the world, separated only by the Congo River.

algeria.name: Algeria
algeria.flagDescription: Two vertical halves — green on the left and white on the right — with a red crescent and star in the center
algeria.famousFor: Being the largest country in Africa by area and having vast stretches of the Sahara Desert

geography.longestRiver: Chelif River — 435 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Tahat — 9,573 feet

famousFood: Couscous — tiny steamed grain served with vegetables and meat, a North African staple
Chakhchoukha — torn flatbread in a spicy tomato and chickpea stew
Makroud — date-filled semolina pastries fried and dipped in honey
festivals: Timgad International Music Festival — a music festival held among ancient Roman ruins in the Sahara
famousPeople: Albert Camus — Nobel Prize-winning author, born in Algeria
Riyad Mahrez — Premier League-winning soccer star
Emir Abdelkader — national hero who resisted colonization in the 1800s
inventions: Ancient Saharan rock art at Tassili n'Ajjer — paintings over 10,000 years old showing life in a green Sahara
Berber architecture — traditional building styles adapted to extreme desert heat
Rai music — a genre blending traditional and modern sounds that became popular worldwide
worldRecords: Algeria is the largest country in Africa by area — about 3.5 times the size of Texas!
The Sahara Desert covers about 80% of Algeria
weirdLaws: In Algeria, driving a dirty car can get you a fine in some cities
uniqueAnimals: Fennec Fox — the world's smallest fox with enormous ears, and Algeria's national animal
Barbary Macaque — one of the few monkey species found in Africa north of the Sahara
Addax — a critically endangered desert antelope
sportsAchievements: Algeria won the Africa Cup of Nations in 2019, sparking massive celebrations
Hassiba Boulmerka won Algeria's first Olympic gold medal in the 1,500 meters at the 1992 Olympics
history: Algeria is the largest country in all of Africa — it's enormous!
The ancient Romans built cities in Algeria, and you can still visit the ruins of Timgad today.
Tassili n'Ajjer has rock paintings over 10,000 years old showing that the Sahara was once green and full of rivers.
Algeria fought a long war for independence from France and became free in 1962.
trivia: About 80% of Algeria is covered by the Sahara Desert — one of the hottest places on Earth.
The Fennec Fox, Algeria's national animal, has giant ears that help it cool off in the desert heat.
Ancient rock art in Algeria shows giraffes and hippos, proving the Sahara was once lush and wet!

zimbabwe.name: Zimbabwe
zimbabwe.flagDescription: Seven horizontal stripes of green, yellow, red, black, red, yellow, green, with a white triangle containing a red star and Zimbabwe Bird on the left
zimbabwe.famousFor: The ancient Great Zimbabwe ruins and Victoria Falls, one of the world's largest waterfalls

geography.longestRiver: Zambezi River — 1,599 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Nyangani — 8,504 feet

famousFood: Sadza — a thick maize porridge that is the staple food, eaten with relishes and stews
Biltong — dried and spiced meat, a popular Southern African snack
Muriwo Unedovi — leafy greens cooked with peanut butter
festivals: Harare International Festival of the Arts (HIFA) — a major celebration of theater, music, dance, and visual arts
famousPeople: King Mutota — founder of the Mutapa Empire in the 1400s
Tsitsi Dangarembga — acclaimed novelist and filmmaker
Kirsty Coventry — Olympic gold medalist swimmer and most decorated Olympian in African history
inventions: Great Zimbabwe — a massive stone city built without mortar, showing incredible ancient engineering
Mbira (thumb piano) — a traditional instrument central to Shona music and culture
Zimbabwe Bird — a famous soapstone carving that became the national symbol
worldRecords: Victoria Falls is one of the largest waterfalls in the world — its spray can be seen from 30 miles away!
Zimbabwe once printed a 100 trillion dollar banknote during its hyperinflation — the highest denomination ever!
weirdLaws: It is illegal to make fun of the president's name in Zimbabwe
uniqueAnimals: Black Rhinoceros — critically endangered and protected in Zimbabwe's reserves
African Elephant — Zimbabwe has some of the largest elephant populations in Africa
Pangolin — a scaly anteater and one of the world's most trafficked animals
sportsAchievements: Kirsty Coventry won 7 Olympic medals in swimming, the most of any African athlete ever
Zimbabwe's cricket team has competed in multiple Cricket World Cups
history: Great Zimbabwe was a massive stone city built between the 1100s and 1400s — the country is named after it!
The name 'Zimbabwe' means 'House of Stone' in the Shona language.
Victoria Falls is called 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' locally, meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders.'
Zimbabwe gained independence in 1980 after a long struggle for majority rule.
trivia: Zimbabwe once printed a 100 trillion dollar bill — it's now a collectors' item!
The mbira (thumb piano) has been played in Zimbabwe for over 1,000 years.
Victoria Falls is so massive that its mist rises high enough to be seen from 30 miles away.

senegal.name: Senegal
senegal.flagDescription: Three vertical stripes — green (left), yellow (center), red (right) — with a green star in the center
senegal.famousFor: Gorée Island's historical significance, vibrant culture, and the tradition of Teranga (hospitality)

geography.longestRiver: Senegal River — 1,086 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Nepen Diakha — 1,906 feet

famousFood: Thieboudienne — the national dish of fish and rice cooked in a rich tomato sauce
Yassa Poulet — chicken marinated in lemon and onions
Mafé — a hearty peanut butter stew served with rice
festivals: Saint-Louis Jazz Festival — one of the biggest jazz festivals in Africa, held in the historic city of Saint-Louis
famousPeople: Léopold Sédar Senghor — first president of Senegal and a famous poet
Sadio Mané — world-famous soccer star who played for Liverpool and Bayern Munich
Youssou N'Dour — Grammy Award-winning musician known worldwide
inventions: Teranga — a cultural tradition of hospitality so strong it's considered part of Senegal's identity
Sabar drumming — a dynamic drumming style central to Senegalese celebrations
Senegalese wrestling (Laamb) — a unique sport combining traditional wrestling with spiritual rituals
worldRecords: The African Renaissance Monument in Dakar stands 160 feet tall — taller than the Statue of Liberty!
Lac Rose (Pink Lake) gets its stunning pink color from special algae, similar to a few other lakes worldwide
weirdLaws: Traditional wrestling matches in Senegal often include mystical rituals and the wrestlers wear special protective charms called gris-gris
uniqueAnimals: West African Manatee — a gentle sea cow found in Senegal's rivers
Green Monkey — a primate commonly found in Senegalese forests
Warthog — a tough wild pig with impressive tusks
sportsAchievements: Senegal reached the quarterfinals of the 2002 FIFA World Cup in their very first appearance, beating France in the opener!
Senegal won their first Africa Cup of Nations title in 2022
history: Gorée Island was a major site in the transatlantic slave trade and is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and memorial.
Senegal gained independence from France in 1960.
The ancient Kingdom of Jolof united much of Senegal from the 1300s to the 1500s.
Senegal is known as the 'Gateway to Africa' because of its position on the western tip of the continent.
trivia: Senegal's national sport isn't soccer — it's traditional wrestling called Laamb!
The African Renaissance Monument in Dakar is taller than the Statue of Liberty.
Senegal is famous for 'Teranga,' meaning hospitality — guests are treated like royalty!

rwanda.name: Rwanda
rwanda.flagDescription: Three horizontal bands — blue (top, taking up half), yellow (middle), and green (bottom) — with a golden sun in the upper right corner
rwanda.famousFor: Being called the 'Land of a Thousand Hills,' mountain gorillas, and its remarkable transformation and recovery

geography.longestRiver: Nyabarongo River — 140 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Karisimbi — 14,787 feet

famousFood: Brochettes — grilled meat skewers, one of the most popular street foods
Isombe — cassava leaves cooked with spinach and peanut paste
Ugali — a thick cornmeal porridge served with beans or meat stew
famousPeople: Dian Fossey — American primatologist who dedicated her life to protecting Rwanda's mountain gorillas
Paul Kagame — president who led Rwanda's remarkable rebuilding
Diane Rwigara — women's rights activist and businesswoman
inventions: Umuganda — a unique national community service day held on the last Saturday of every month where everyone helps clean and build
Drone delivery network — Rwanda became one of the first countries to use drones to deliver medical supplies to remote hospitals
Plastic bag ban — Rwanda was one of the first countries in the world to ban single-use plastic bags, making it one of the cleanest nations on Earth
worldRecords: Rwanda is considered one of the cleanest countries in the world thanks to its nationwide cleanup days
Rwanda has the highest percentage of women in parliament of any country in the world — over 60%!
weirdLaws: Plastic bags are completely banned in Rwanda — if you bring one into the country, it will be confiscated at the border!
uniqueAnimals: Mountain Gorilla — fewer than 1,000 remain in the wild, and many live in Rwanda's Volcanoes National Park
Golden Monkey — a beautiful and playful endangered primate found in Rwanda's bamboo forests
Grey Crowned Crane — an elegant bird that is one of Rwanda's national symbols
sportsAchievements: Rwanda has become a rising force in professional cycling, with Team Rwanda inspiring a new generation of cyclists
The country hosts the Tour du Rwanda, a major international cycling race
history: Rwanda is called the 'Land of a Thousand Hills' because of its beautiful rolling green landscape.
In 1994, Rwanda experienced a devastating genocide, but the country has made a remarkable recovery.
Rwanda has rebuilt itself into one of the safest, cleanest, and fastest-growing countries in Africa.
Dian Fossey lived among mountain gorillas in Rwanda's volcanoes and helped save them from extinction.
trivia: Rwanda has more women in parliament than any other country in the world — over 60%!
Every last Saturday of the month, all Rwandans participate in Umuganda — a national community cleanup day.
Rwanda was one of the first countries to ban plastic bags, making it super clean!

madagascar.name: Madagascar
madagascar.flagDescription: A white vertical stripe on the left, with red (top) and green (bottom) horizontal stripes on the right
madagascar.famousFor: Lemurs and unique animals found nowhere else on Earth

geography.longestRiver: Mangoky River — 350 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Maromokotro — 9,436 feet

famousFood: Romazava — the national dish, a stew of meat and leafy greens
Ravitoto — pork cooked with shredded cassava leaves and coconut
Mofo Gasy — sweet rice flour cakes cooked on the street, a popular breakfast
festivals: Famadihana — a unique tradition where families dig up the remains of their ancestors, rewrap them, and dance with them to celebrate their memory
famousPeople: Philibert Tsiranana — first president of independent Madagascar
Jean-Joseph Rabearivelo — one of the greatest poets from Africa
Lanto Rakotomavo — pioneering conservationist who worked to protect lemurs
inventions: Vanilla cultivation — Madagascar produces about 80% of the world's vanilla!
Lamba cloth — traditional woven fabric used in ceremonies and daily life
Valiha — a tube-shaped bamboo instrument, the national instrument of Madagascar
worldRecords: Madagascar is the fourth-largest island in the world!
About 90% of Madagascar's wildlife is found nowhere else on Earth
weirdLaws: In some Malagasy communities, it is fady (taboo) to point at a grave or a rainbow
uniqueAnimals: Lemur — over 100 species of lemurs live only in Madagascar
Fossa — a cat-like predator that is the largest carnivore on the island
Panther Chameleon — a stunning color-changing lizard found only in Madagascar
sportsAchievements: Madagascar's national rugby team, the Makis (named after a type of lemur!), competes in the Africa Cup
The country has a passionate tradition of pétanque, a bowling-like sport inherited from French culture
history: Madagascar split from mainland Africa over 160 million years ago, which is why its animals are so unique!
The island was first settled by people from Southeast Asia — over 3,500 miles across the Indian Ocean — around 2,000 years ago.
Madagascar gained independence from France in 1960.
The Merina Kingdom unified much of Madagascar in the 1800s and had a powerful queen, Ranavalona I.
trivia: About 90% of the animals and plants in Madagascar are found NOWHERE else on the planet!
Madagascar produces about 80% of the world's vanilla — so your ice cream probably came from here.
The Avenue of the Baobabs features ancient trees that are over 800 years old and look like they're planted upside down!

nepal.name: Nepal
nepal.flagDescription: The only non-rectangular national flag in the world — two stacked red triangles with blue borders, featuring a white moon and sun
nepal.famousFor: Mount Everest, the Himalayas, and the only non-rectangular national flag in the world

geography.longestRiver: Karnali River — 507 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Mount Everest — 29,032 feet (the tallest mountain on Earth!)

famousFood: Momo — steamed dumplings filled with meat or vegetables, Nepal's favorite snack
Dal Bhat — lentil soup with rice, eaten twice a day by most Nepalis
Sel Roti — a crispy ring-shaped rice donut made during festivals
festivals: Dashain — Nepal's biggest festival, a 15-day celebration of the victory of good over evil
Holi — the festival of colors, where people throw colored powder at each other
famousPeople: Tenzing Norgay — one of the first two people to reach the top of Mount Everest in 1953
Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha) — founder of Buddhism, born in Lumbini, Nepal
Apa Sherpa — climbed Mount Everest 21 times, holding the record for years
inventions: Birthplace of Buddha and the origin of Buddhism
Sherpa mountaineering expertise that made Himalayan climbing possible
The Nepali calendar (Bikram Sambat), one of the oldest calendar systems still in use
worldRecords: Home to Mount Everest, the tallest mountain on Earth at 29,032 feet
The only country in the world with a non-rectangular flag
weirdLaws: Cows are sacred in Nepal and it is illegal to slaughter them — the cow is actually the national animal!
uniqueAnimals: Red Panda — a cute, cat-sized animal with reddish fur found in Nepal's forests
Snow Leopard — a rare, ghost-like big cat that lives high in the Himalayas
One-horned Rhinoceros — an endangered rhino found in Nepal's lowland jungles
sportsAchievements: Nepal has produced world-class mountaineers, including Nirmal Purja who climbed all 14 peaks over 8,000 meters in just 6 months
Cricket is hugely popular and Nepal earned ODI (One Day International) status in 2018
history: Nepal is one of the oldest countries in Asia and was never colonized by any foreign power.
Siddhartha Gautama, who became the Buddha, was born in Lumbini, Nepal around 563 BC.
The word 'Everest' comes from Sir George Everest, but Nepalis call it 'Sagarmatha' meaning 'Goddess of the Sky.'
Nepal was a monarchy for centuries until it became a republic in 2008.
trivia: Nepal's flag is the only national flag that isn't rectangular — it's made of two stacked triangles!
Eight of the world's ten tallest mountains are in Nepal.
Nepali time is one of the few time zones offset by 45 minutes (UTC+5:45).

mongolia.name: Mongolia
mongolia.flagDescription: Three vertical stripes — red, blue, red — with a golden Soyombo symbol on the left red stripe
mongolia.nationalSport: Wrestling, archery, and horse racing (the 'Three Manly Games')
mongolia.famousFor: Genghis Khan, vast open steppes, nomadic culture, and eagle hunting
landmarks: Genghis Khan Equestrian Statue — the world's tallest equestrian statue at 131 feet
Erdene Zuu Monastery — Mongolia's oldest surviving Buddhist monastery

geography.longestRiver: Orkhon River — 698 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Khüiten Peak — 14,350 feet

famousFood: Buuz — steamed dumplings filled with meat, eaten during Lunar New Year celebrations
Khorkhog — meat cooked with hot stones inside a metal container, a traditional barbecue
Airag — fermented mare's milk, a traditional Mongolian drink
festivals: Naadam Festival — a huge celebration featuring the 'Three Manly Games': wrestling, horse racing, and archery
Tsagaan Sar — Mongolian Lunar New Year, a three-day celebration with family visits and feasting
famousPeople: Genghis Khan — founded the largest land empire in history, stretching from China to Europe
Kublai Khan — grandson of Genghis Khan who became Emperor of China and founded the Yuan Dynasty
Khutulun — a legendary Mongolian princess who was an undefeated wrestler
inventions: The Mongolian horse relay postal system (Yam), one of the earliest mail delivery networks
The ger (yurt) — a portable felt tent perfected by Mongolian nomads
Composite bow — a powerful curved bow used by Mongol warriors that could shoot over 500 yards
worldRecords: The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history, covering 24 million square kilometers
Mongolia is the most sparsely populated country in the world — about 2 people per square kilometer
weirdLaws: In Mongolia, it is considered very rude and bad luck to step on a threshold when entering a ger (yurt) — you must always step over it
uniqueAnimals: Przewalski's Horse — the last truly wild horse species, saved from extinction in Mongolia
Gobi Bear — one of the rarest bears in the world, with fewer than 50 left in the wild
Golden Eagle — trained by Kazakh eagle hunters in western Mongolia to hunt foxes and wolves
sportsAchievements: Mongolia has won Olympic medals in judo, boxing, and shooting
Mongolian wrestlers (sumo) have dominated Japan's sumo wrestling, with several becoming grand champions (yokozuna)
history: Genghis Khan united the Mongolian tribes in 1206 and built the largest land empire ever.
The Mongol Empire connected East and West through trade routes, helping create the Silk Road.
About 30% of Mongolians still live as nomads, moving with their herds across the steppe.
Mongolia was under Chinese and then Soviet influence for centuries before becoming a democracy in 1990.
trivia: Mongolia has more horses than people — about 4 million horses for 3.4 million people!
Kazakh eagle hunters in western Mongolia train golden eagles to hunt, a tradition over 4,000 years old.
Mongolian kids learn to ride horses before they can walk — some start riding at age 3!

panama.name: Panama
panama.flagDescription: Four rectangles — white with blue star (top left), red (top right), blue (bottom left), white with red star (bottom right)
panama.famousFor: The Panama Canal, connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, and incredible biodiversity
landmarks: Panama Canal — one of the greatest engineering achievements in history
Casco Viejo — the historic old quarter of Panama City
San Blas Islands — a tropical archipelago of over 360 islands

geography.longestRiver: Chucunaque River — 144 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Volcán Barú — 11,398 feet

famousFood: Sancocho — a hearty chicken soup with root vegetables, considered Panama's national dish
Ceviche — fresh raw fish cured in citrus juice with onions and peppers
Hojaldras — fried dough served for breakfast, like a Panamanian donut
festivals: Carnival of Panama — one of the biggest carnival celebrations in Central America, with parades, music, and water trucks
famousPeople: Mariano Rivera — greatest baseball closer of all time, Hall of Famer from Panama
Roberto Durán — legendary boxer known as 'Hands of Stone'
Rod Carew — baseball Hall of Famer born in a train car on the Panama Canal Zone
inventions: The Panama Canal — a 50-mile waterway that saves ships a 12,000-mile trip around South America
The Panama hat (which was actually popularized in Panama, though woven in Ecuador)
Panama was the first Latin American country to adopt the US dollar as legal currency
worldRecords: The Panama Canal moves about 14,000 ships per year, carrying 5% of the world's trade
Panama is the only place in the world where you can watch the sun rise over the Pacific and set over the Atlantic
weirdLaws: It is illegal to swim in the Panama Canal — you could get a hefty fine for taking a dip!
uniqueAnimals: Harpy Eagle — one of the largest and most powerful eagles in the world, Panama's national bird
Panamanian Golden Frog — a tiny, bright yellow frog that is a national symbol
Three-toed Sloth — slow-moving tree dwellers found in Panama's rainforests
sportsAchievements: Mariano Rivera is the only player in MLB history to be unanimously voted into the Hall of Fame
Panama has qualified for the FIFA World Cup (2018) and regularly competes in the World Baseball Classic
history: The Panama Canal opened in 1914 and took 10 years and over 75,000 workers to build.
Panama was part of Colombia until it declared independence in 1903.
The country is a land bridge connecting North and South America, allowing animals to migrate between continents.
Panama took full control of the Panama Canal from the United States on December 31, 1999.
trivia: Panama is the only place where you can see the sun rise on the Pacific Ocean and set on the Atlantic — because the country curves!
Panama has more bird species than the United States and Canada combined.
The Panama Canal uses a system of locks that lift ships 85 feet above sea level to cross between oceans.

botswana.name: Botswana
botswana.flagDescription: A light blue background with a horizontal black stripe bordered by thin white stripes through the center
botswana.famousFor: The Okavango Delta, diamond mining, safari wildlife, and the Kalahari Desert
landmarks: Okavango Delta — the world's largest inland delta, a massive oasis in the desert
Chobe National Park — home to the largest elephant population in Africa
Makgadikgadi Salt Pans — enormous flat salt pans visible from space

geography.longestRiver: Okavango River — 1,000 miles (it never reaches the sea — it flows into the desert!)
geography.tallestPeak: Otse Hill — 4,886 feet

famousFood: Seswaa — shredded, slow-cooked beef or goat, Botswana's national dish
Bogobe — a thick porridge made from sorghum or maize, a staple food
Vetkoek — deep-fried dough balls, sometimes stuffed with mince meat
famousPeople: Sir Seretse Khama — Botswana's first president who transformed the country from one of the poorest to a success story
Mpule Kwelagobe — Miss Universe 1999, the first from Botswana
Unity Dow — first female High Court judge in Botswana and a human rights champion
inventions: Botswana is a model for how to manage diamond wealth responsibly for the benefit of citizens
The country created one of Africa's most successful wildlife conservation programs
Botswana pioneered community-based natural resource management in Africa
worldRecords: Botswana has the highest concentration of African elephants in the world — over 130,000
The Okavango Delta is the world's largest inland river delta
weirdLaws: In Botswana, it is illegal to take a photograph of government buildings without permission
uniqueAnimals: African Wild Dog — one of the most endangered carnivores, found in Botswana's reserves
Kalahari Lion — black-maned lions adapted to life in the harsh Kalahari Desert
Meerkats — small, social mammals that stand upright on their hind legs as lookouts
sportsAchievements: Nijel Amos won a silver medal in the 800 meters at the 2012 Olympics — a huge achievement for a small country
Botswana's 4x400m relay team won gold at the 2024 Paris Olympics
history: When Botswana gained independence in 1966, it was one of the poorest countries in the world.
The discovery of diamonds in 1967 transformed Botswana into one of Africa's wealthiest nations.
Botswana has been a stable democracy since independence — one of the longest in Africa.
The Kalahari Desert covers about 70% of the country and is home to the San people, one of the oldest cultures on Earth.
trivia: Botswana's currency is called the 'Pula,' which means 'rain' — because rain is so precious in this dry country!
The Okavango Delta floods every year, turning dry land into a lush paradise for wildlife.
Botswana has more elephants than any other country in Africa.

jordan.name: Jordan
jordan.flagDescription: Three horizontal stripes — black, white, green — with a red triangle on the left side containing a white seven-pointed star
jordan.famousFor: Petra (the ancient rock-carved city), the Dead Sea, and rich ancient history
landmarks: Petra — an ancient city carved into rose-red cliffs, one of the New Seven Wonders of the World
The Dead Sea — the lowest point on Earth and the saltiest body of water
Wadi Rum — a stunning red desert valley also known as the Valley of the Moon

geography.longestRiver: Jordan River — 156 miles
geography.tallestPeak: Jabal Umm ad Dami — 6,083 feet

famousFood: Mansaf — lamb cooked in yogurt sauce and served over rice, Jordan's national dish
Falafel — crispy fried balls made from chickpeas, a popular street food
Knafeh — a sweet cheese pastry soaked in sugar syrup, a beloved dessert
festivals: Jerash Festival — a major cultural festival held in the ancient Roman city of Jerash with music, dance, and theater
famousPeople: Queen Rania — one of the world's most admired queens, known for education and children's rights
King Hussein — ruled Jordan for 46 years and was a champion of peace in the Middle East
Mustafa Wahbi Tal (Arar) — Jordan's most famous poet
inventions: The Nabataeans of Petra built one of the ancient world's most advanced water systems
Jordan preserves some of the world's oldest mosaics, including the Madaba Map from the 6th century
Ancient Jordanian civilizations contributed to early agriculture and irrigation techniques
worldRecords: The Dead Sea is the lowest point on Earth at 1,412 feet below sea level
The Dead Sea is so salty (34% salt) that you float on the surface without even trying
weirdLaws: In the Dead Sea, the water is so buoyant that it's actually very hard to swim normally — you just bob on the surface like a cork!
uniqueAnimals: Arabian Oryx — a beautiful white antelope that was brought back from near extinction
